proposals.title: A Modified Generalized Scaling Law for Seismic Pore Pressure Generation in Partially Saturated Slopes
proposals.motivation: The Generalized Scaling Law (GSL) is a key framework for predicting pore pressure rise and liquefaction triggering in fully saturated soils under cyclic loading. However, natural slopes are often partially saturated due to rainfall infiltration and drainage, where the presence of air significantly alters the pore fluid compressibility and pressure response. Current practice either ignores this effect or uses overly simplified corrections, limiting the accuracy of seismic slope stability assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The GSL can be extended to partially saturated conditions by incorporating a compressible pore fluid model, where the degree of saturation directly governs the rate of seismic pore pressure generation and the onset of liquefaction or strength loss in sloping ground.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled numerical-experimental approach. First, a series of dynamic centrifuge tests will be conducted on instrumented sand slopes with controlled, spatially varying degrees of saturation. Second, a coupled finite element framework, building on the researcher's prior work, will be developed. This framework will implement a three-phase (soil-water-air) u-p-U formulation, integrating the relative fluid acceleration and a constitutive model linking saturation to the bulk modulus of the pore fluid. The modified GSL will be derived and calibrated by back-analyzing the centrifuge data using this numerical model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, physics-based scaling relationship for pore pressure generation in unsaturated soils, directly applicable to performance-based seismic design. It will yield practical guidelines for when partial saturation can be safely ignored or must be explicitly considered in liquefaction hazard evaluation for slopes.
proposals.novelty: While the GSL is well-established for saturated soils and centrifuge modeling of unsaturated flow is known, the explicit integration of a dynamic three-phase constitutive model into the GSL framework for seismic slope analysis is novel. This bridges the gap between unsaturated soil mechanics and dynamic geotechnical engineering.

proposals.title: Validation of a Generalized Scaling Law for Seismic Pore Pressure Generation Across Soil Types and Densities
proposals.motivation: The Generalized Scaling Law (GSL) offers a promising unified framework for predicting pore pressure generation during seismic loading, but its validation has been limited to specific sands and densities. For practical application in performance-based design and liquefaction hazard assessment, its robustness across a wider range of natural soils (e.g., silty sands, residual soils) and initial states (loose to dense) must be rigorously tested. This is critical for extending its use to diverse geotechnical settings, including the sloping ground and layered deposits often studied by the researcher.
proposals.hypothesis: The Generalized Scaling Law can accurately model pore pressure generation for a broad spectrum of soil types and initial relative densities when calibrated with appropriate cyclic resistance parameters. Its predictive capability will degrade for soils with significant fines content or complex fabric unless the scaling parameters are explicitly linked to soil index properties and state.
proposals.method: The research will employ a combined experimental and numerical calibration approach. A suite of cyclic direct simple shear and triaxial tests will be performed on materials including clean sand, silty sand, and a residual soil, prepared at a range of initial relative densities. Test data (cyclic stress ratio, number of cycles to liquefaction) will be used to calibrate the GSL parameters for each soil-density combination. The calibrated laws will be validated against independent test data and implemented in coupled finite element analyses (extending the researcher's prior work on poroelastic dynamics) to simulate centrifuge model tests of level and sloping ground, comparing predicted and measured pore pressure response and reliquefaction behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated database of GSL parameters for common soil types, significantly expanding the law's applicability. It will deliver practical guidelines for selecting scaling parameters based on soil classification and state, moving the GSL from a conceptual framework to an engineering tool. The results will directly inform more reliable coupled numerical simulations of seismic soil-structure interaction on complex topography.
proposals.novelty: While the GSL has been explored conceptually, this systematic experimental validation across soil types and densities, integrated with advanced coupled numerical modeling, is novel. It uniquely bridges high-quality laboratory characterization, centrifuge physics, and computational geomechanics to test the limits of a unified pore pressure model.

proposals.title: Full-Field Measurement of Rainfall-Induced Slope Deformation and Preferential Flow Paths using Digital Image Correlation in Geotechnical Centrifuge Modelling
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced slope failures are a major geohazard, but current understanding of the coupled hydromechanical processes—infiltration, suction loss, and the initiation of shear bands—is limited by point-based measurements. High-g centrifuge tests realistically replicate stress fields, yet they lack techniques to capture the full-field, non-uniform soil deformation and water flow patterns that precede failure, especially in complex, unsaturated residual soils.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying high-resolution 2D Digital Image Correlation (DIC) to centrifuge model slopes will reveal localized, transient deformation patterns and the development of preferential infiltration paths that are not detectable with conventional instrumentation, providing a direct link between infiltration heterogeneity and failure initiation.
proposals.method: We will adapt established DIC algorithms (e.g., normalized cross-correlation or Fourier-based methods) from biomechanics and experimental solid mechanics for geotechnical use. A transparent strongbox with a seeded soil pattern (e.g., dyed sand speckles) will be subjected to simulated rainfall in a geotechnical centrifuge. High-speed cameras mounted on the centrifuge package will capture images during shaking (reliquefaction) and steady infiltration. The DIC software (e.g., Ncorr or a custom MATLAB/Python code using OpenCV) will compute full-field displacement vectors and strain maps by tracking the speckle pattern subset movement between frames.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first full-field, quantitative visualization of deformation and flow coupling in physical slope models under enhanced gravity. It will validate and improve coupled finite element poroelastic models by providing high-resolution data on boundary conditions and internal strain evolution. The methodology will establish a new standard for spatially dense measurement in physical geotechnics.
proposals.novelty: While DIC is common in biomechanics and material science, its application to dynamic, unsaturated soil behavior in a geotechnical centrifuge environment—particularly to simultaneously resolve deformation and infer flow paths during rainfall infiltration—is novel and bridges a critical gap between point measurements and continuum-scale numerical models.

proposals.title: Coupled Hydro-Mechanical Modeling of Earthquake-Induced Landslide Dam Stability: Integrating Rainfall Infiltration, Seepage, and Progressive Failure
proposals.motivation: The 2024 Noto Peninsula earthquake in Japan created over 50 landslide dams, posing a severe, long-term secondary hazard of catastrophic outburst floods. Pre-2026 risk assessment methods often treat these dams as static, homogeneous masses, neglecting the coupled effects of rainfall infiltration, internal seepage, and material degradation over time, which are critical for predicting delayed failures.
proposals.hypothesis: The long-term stability of earthquake-induced landslide dams is governed by a time-dependent, coupled hydro-mechanical process where rainfall infiltration alters pore pressures and seepage paths, leading to progressive internal erosion and strength reduction, ultimately controlling the timing and mode of failure.
proposals.method: We will develop a 3D coupled finite element model using a modified u-p-U formulation (considering relative fluid acceleration) to simulate dynamic seepage and deformation. The model will incorporate site-specific soil properties from field infiltration tests (e.g., double-ring infiltrometer data) to calibrate unsaturated flow parameters. Slope stability will be evaluated using a time-integrated strength reduction technique, modeling progressive failure under cyclic rainfall scenarios representative of the region's climate.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a predictive framework for assessing the time-dependent failure risk of landslide dams, moving beyond static factor-of-safety approaches. It will deliver a calibrated, open-source numerical tool for geotechnical engineers and hazard planners to prioritize monitoring and mitigation efforts for the most vulnerable dams.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly coupling the dynamics of post-seismic rainfall infiltration and internal seepage with progressive failure mechanics in a 3D model tailored for the heterogeneous, unsaturated materials typical of landslide dams, a synthesis not commonly addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Dynamic Failure Mechanisms of Unsaturated Volcanic Slopes: A Shaking-Table Study of Infiltration-Induced Pore Pressure and Strength Degradation
proposals.motivation: Volcanic soils, characterized by crushable pumice sands and allophanic clays, exhibit complex hydro-mechanical behavior that is poorly represented in standard liquefaction and slope stability frameworks. The failure of natural and engineered slopes on volcanic terrain during seismic-rainfall sequences, as observed in past events, poses a significant hazard, yet the coupled effects of dynamic shaking, unsaturated infiltration, and soil fabric collapse remain inadequately quantified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the seismic performance of unsaturated volcanic soil slopes is governed by a critical interplay between the rate of rainfall infiltration, the generation of excess pore pressure during shaking, and the unique compressibility of the soil skeleton, leading to failure modes distinct from those in typical silica sands.
proposals.method: We will conduct 1-g shaking-table tests on instrumented, scaled slope models constructed from characterized volcanic soils (e.g., Toyoura sand mixed with crushable pumice or reconstituted allophane). Tests will follow a coupled experimental protocol: first, controlled rainfall simulation via a misting system to establish varying initial unsaturated moisture profiles; second, application of scaled earthquake motions with varying PGA. We will employ the coupled finite element method, considering relative fluid acceleration as implemented in prior work, to design model similitude and interpret the dynamics of the poroelastic media. Key measurements include pore water pressure (using miniature tensiometers and pressure transducers), acceleration, and displacement via high-speed imaging and particle image velocimetry (PIV).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel experimental dataset on the coupled hydromechanical response of unsaturated volcanic soils under dynamic loading. It will produce improved failure envelopes and inform the refinement of constitutive models for volcanic materials, directly contributing to more reliable seismic slope stability assessment in volcanic regions.
proposals.novelty: While liquefaction of saturated sands and stability of residual soils have been studied, this proposal uniquely integrates dynamic shaking with controlled unsaturated infiltration specifically for crushable, high-porosity volcanic soils, bridging the gap between the researcher's expertise in infiltration, reliquefaction, and poroelastic dynamics.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Pre-Failure Shear Strength Parameters in Rainfall-Triggered Landslides via Coupled Hydro-Mechanical Back-Analysis of Slide Geometry
proposals.motivation: Post-failure site investigations for landslides often find the in-situ soil strength has been altered, making direct measurement of pre-failure properties impossible. This creates a critical gap in calibrating regional slope stability models and understanding failure initiation, especially for rainfall-induced slides in unsaturated residual soils. A method to reliably back-calculate the pre-failure strength from observable final geometry would significantly improve hazard assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The mobilized shear strength at the onset of failure for a rainfall-triggered landslide can be uniquely constrained by inverting the coupled hydro-mechanical processes that link the transient pore pressure field, slope geometry, and the final failed mass.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled finite element model, building on Uzuoka's prior work, to simulate unsaturated infiltration dynamics and slope deformation. The inverse problem will be solved using a Bayesian framework. Observables (final slide scar geometry, depth, and volume from LiDAR) will be inputs. The model will iteratively adjust the pre-failure effective cohesion and friction angle via a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) algorithm until the simulated failure geometry matches the observed one, constrained by realistic infiltration boundary conditions derived from rainfall records.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physics-based methodology to estimate in-situ geotechnical properties where direct sampling is impossible. It will advance the field of forensic geotechnics by quantitatively linking hydrology and mechanics in back-analysis. The resulting parameter distributions will improve the calibration of regional slope stability models for residual soils.
proposals.novelty: While forward stability analysis is standard, this proposal uniquely integrates a coupled unsaturated flow-deformation model within a Bayesian inversion scheme specifically for strength parameter estimation. It moves beyond simple limit equilibrium back-analysis by explicitly modeling the dynamic infiltration process that leads to failure.

proposals.title: A Fracture Mechanics Framework for Seismic Crack Initiation and Propagation in Unsaturated Slopes
proposals.motivation: Earthquake-induced ground failure in slopes often initiates from the propagation of desiccation or shaking-induced cracks, which dramatically alter soil's hydraulic and mechanical response. Pre-2026 models for slope stability and liquefaction potential, including those used in centrifuge tests, typically treat soil as a continuum, inadequately capturing the discrete, progressive nature of crack growth and its feedback on pore pressure dynamics during seismic shaking. This gap limits predictive capability for rainfall-infiltration-triggered failures post-earthquake.
proposals.hypothesis: The initiation and propagation of cracks in unsaturated soil slopes under seismic loading can be modeled using a mixed-mode (I/II) fracture mechanics approach, where the crack-tip stress intensity factors are governed by coupled seismic pore pressure generation and soil suction dynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a coupled finite element model using a cohesive zone fracture mechanics approach integrated into a u-p (displacement-pore pressure) formulation for unsaturated porous media. The model will implement a fatigue-type crack propagation law, where the crack driving force is a function of the cyclic shear stress intensity and the transient pore water pressure at the crack tip simulated during seismic loading. The framework will be validated against centrifuge model test data of sand reliquefaction on slopes and field infiltration data from residual soils.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a physics-based tool to predict the evolution of soil fabric damage during earthquakes, linking discrete fracture to bulk hydromechanical response. It will advance the assessment of post-seismic landslide and reliquefaction hazard by quantifying how crack networks accelerate preferential infiltration and weaken soil structure.
proposals.novelty: While fracture mechanics is standard for concrete and rock, its application to model dynamic crack growth in unsaturated, cohesionless soils under seismic loading is novel. This approach moves beyond empirical correlations or continuum damage models by explicitly modeling the fracture process zone and its interaction with pore fluids.

proposals.title: Bayesian Data Assimilation of Geophysical Surveys for Probabilistic Slope Stability Analysis Under Rainfall and Seismic Loading
proposals.motivation: Slope stability assessments are critically sensitive to subsurface heterogeneity in soil properties, which is prohibitively expensive to characterize through dense borehole sampling. This leads to high uncertainty in failure predictions, especially under combined rainfall infiltration and seismic shaking. A practical framework is needed to integrate cost-effective, spatially extensive geophysical data with physical models to quantify this uncertainty and guide risk-informed decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: That a Bayesian data assimilation framework, which inverts electrical resistivity tomography (ERT) and seismic refraction data to condition spatially correlated random fields of hydraulic conductivity and shear strength, can significantly reduce the predictive uncertainty of coupled hydromechanical slope stability models compared to traditional homogeneous or sparsely sampled approaches.
proposals.method: The method integrates field-scale ERT surveys with laboratory-derived petrophysical relationships (e.g., Archie's law, porosity-permeability correlations) within a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) inversion scheme to generate an ensemble of possible subsurface realizations. These realizations, represented as random fields with Matérn covariance, will constrain parameters for a coupled finite element model (extending Uzuoka's prior work on poroelastic dynamics) simulating pore pressure response to rainfall and seismic loading. Slope stability will be evaluated probabilistically using the Factor of Safety distribution from the ensemble.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source computational framework for geophysically-constrained probabilistic slope assessment, directly linking non-invasive sensing to engineering risk metrics. It will quantify how different geophysical data types reduce uncertainty in failure predictions, offering practitioners a methodology to optimize site investigation campaigns for slope safety.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight coupling of a Bayesian geophysical inversion specifically for conditioning spatially correlated random fields of *both* hydraulic and mechanical properties, and the subsequent use of these fields in a fully coupled dynamic poroelastic slope stability model, moving beyond simple limit equilibrium or uncoupled analyses.

proposals.title: A Micromechanics-Based Homogenization Framework for Slope Stability Analysis of Heterogeneous Soils Under Rainfall Infiltration
proposals.motivation: Conventional slope stability models often assume uniform soil layers, which fails to capture the complex, heterogeneous reality of natural slopes containing inclusions like clay lenses or gravel layers. This heterogeneity critically controls localized infiltration, pore pressure development, and ultimately, failure mechanisms. A physics-based method to upscale this microstructural complexity is needed to improve the accuracy of landslide hazard prediction, especially under dynamic rainfall and seismic conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The macroscopic hydromechanical response of a heterogeneous slope soil can be accurately derived by treating it as a composite material and applying computational homogenization to a defined Soil Representative Volume Element (SRVE). This will yield spatially variable, anisotropic effective permeability and strength tensors that govern slope-scale stability.
proposals.method: The research will adapt composite material micromechanics to geotechnical problems. First, define SRVEs from field/experimental data characterizing inclusions (e.g., sand, clay lenses) within a soil matrix. Then, implement a two-scale computational homogenization scheme using the finite element method (FEM). At the micro-scale, periodic boundary conditions will be applied to SRVEs to solve coupled hydro-mechanical boundary value problems (using Biot-type poroelasticity). The computed micro-scale responses will be homogenized to derive effective, anisotropic constitutive tensors (permeability, elasticity, strength) for use in macro-scale slope stability simulations under varied infiltration and shaking conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, microstructure-informed framework for slope stability analysis that moves beyond empirical averaging. It will deliver a validated methodology to derive physics-based anisotropic material properties for heterogeneous soils, directly improving the prediction of preferential flow paths, pore pressure buildup, and failure initiation. The framework will be integrated into coupled finite element codes for dynamic analysis of poroelastic media.
proposals.novelty: The direct analogy from composite material micromechanics to soil heterogeneity for slope stability is novel. While homogenization is used in geomechanics, its application to derive anisotropic, hydromechanically coupled effective properties for natural slope soils from defined SRVEs represents a significant, non-obvious advancement over traditional layered or random field approaches.

proposals.title: Numerical Investigation of Rainfall-Induced Slope Instability Considering Heterogeneous Soil Layers and Seismic Preconditioning
proposals.motivation: Conventional slope stability analyses often assume uniform soil layers, which oversimplifies subsurface heterogeneity. This is critical because preferential flow paths, low-permeability lenses, or relic liquefied sand layers from past earthquakes can drastically alter pore pressure dynamics during rainfall, leading to unexpected failures. A coupled assessment of seismic history and subsequent rainfall infiltration on heterogeneous slopes remains a significant gap in geohazard prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the presence of discontinuous, low-permeability layers or relic liquefaction zones within a slope will create localized zones of elevated pore pressure during rainfall, significantly reducing the factor of safety compared to a uniform layer model, especially when dynamic soil weakening from prior shaking is considered.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled finite element analysis (FEA) of the dynamics of poroelastic media, extending R. Uzuoka's prior work to model transient, unsaturated infiltration. The domain will incorporate heterogeneous soil inclusions (e.g., clay lenses in residual soil, denser sand pockets) with spatially variable hydraulic properties. The analysis will be staged: first, simulating seismic shaking and potential reliquefaction in sand layers using centrifuge-informed constitutive models; second, simulating rainfall infiltration under different slope conditions. The response will be evaluated using the finite element software COMSOL Multiphysics or a custom code implementing the u-p formulation with the relative fluid acceleration term.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated numerical framework for assessing compound seismic-rainfall hazards on realistic, heterogeneous slopes. It will yield new insights into how subsurface discontinuities control the timing and location of failure initiation. The results will inform improved geotechnical design and hazard zonation by moving beyond the uniform layer assumption.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit coupling of seismic preconditioning (sand reliquefaction behavior) with subsequent heterogeneous infiltration analysis in a single, dynamic poroelastic framework, directly challenging the uniform layer paradigm for slope stability.

proposals.title: A Physics-Informed Neural Network for Slope Failure Timing, Trained on a Grain-Scale Digital Twin of a Centrifuge Model
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced slope failures are a major geohazard, but predicting their precise timing remains a critical challenge. Existing continuum models often rely on simplified constitutive laws that cannot capture the micromechanical transitions—such as particle rearrangement and pore pressure localization—that dictate the exact moment of failure initiation observed in physical experiments. Bridging this gap between grain-scale mechanisms and field-scale predictive models is essential for improving early warning systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A high-fidelity grain-scale digital twin of a centrifuge slope test, coupling Discrete Element Method (DEM) with pore-scale fluid flow, can generate a synthetic dataset that, when used to train a physics-informed machine learning model, will yield a reduced-order predictor capable of accurately forecasting failure timing from initial soil state and rainfall intensity.
proposals.method: We will first construct a digital twin by calibrating a coupled DEM (using YADE or LIGGGHTS) and pore-scale fluid solver (e.g., Lattice Boltzmann Method or resolved CFD) against Dr. Uzuoka's existing centrifuge test data on sand slopes. This model will explicitly simulate particle-fluid dynamics under varied rainfall intensities. The validated twin will generate a comprehensive synthetic dataset of failure timings. This dataset will train a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) architecture, where the loss function incorporates governing physical constraints (e.g., mass conservation, Darcy's law) to ensure predictions are physically plausible, creating a fast, reduced-order surrogate model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, micromechanically-grounded framework for predicting slope failure timing, moving beyond purely empirical or continuum-based approaches. It will produce both a validated high-resolution digital twin for fundamental study and a practical, physics-informed machine learning tool that can be adapted for field-scale forecasting scenarios.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a fully coupled grain-fluid digital twin as the data generator for a physics-informed surrogate model is a novel synthesis of high-fidelity simulation and machine learning for geotechnical problems. This approach directly links observable macro-failure to simulated micro-mechanisms in a way that standard constitutive model calibration does not.

proposals.title: A Cascading Reservoir Model for Perched Water Table Dynamics and Its Impact on Slope Stability in Residual Soils
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced landslides on slopes with a soil-bedrock interface are often triggered by the formation of a transient perched water table, which drastically reduces effective stress. Pre-2026, while the physics of infiltration and pore pressure response are well-studied, simplified, predictive models that explicitly capture the threshold-driven formation and downslope flow of perched water are lacking. This gap hinders efficient, regional-scale assessment of slope failure risk under variable rainfall conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The dynamics of a perched water table at a soil-bedrock capillary barrier can be accurately modeled by adapting a two-reservoir cascade concept, where the onset of perched flow and its downslope transmission are governed by storage thresholds and release functions analogous to surface watershed runoff.
proposals.method: We will develop a 1D-2D coupled model. The vertical soil profile is discretized into two cascading reservoirs: an upper soil moisture reservoir (SMR) governed by Green-Ampt or Richards equation for infiltration, and a lower perched zone reservoir (PZR) that fills from drainage exceeding the capillary barrier's holding capacity (parameterized from soil-water characteristic curves). The PZR releases water via a downslope routing function based on the slope angle and the saturated hydraulic conductivity of the interface material. The model will be implemented in a finite element framework for poroelastic media (extending Uzuoka's prior work) to couple the hydrological reservoir outputs with dynamic changes in pore-water pressure and effective stress for stability analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, computationally efficient framework to predict the timing and magnitude of perched water table development from antecedent rainfall. It will directly link rainfall patterns to instability by quantifying the transient pore-pressure response within the slope. The model will be validated against centrifuge test data for sand reliquefaction and field infiltration data from residual soils, enhancing predictive capability for rainfall-triggered landslides.
proposals.novelty: The explicit adaptation of surface hydrological reservoir cascade theory to subsurface perched water dynamics represents a novel conceptual analogy. It moves beyond complex, fully 3D variably saturated flow models by using threshold-based storage-release rules to capture the non-linear initiation of perched flow, a simplification not previously applied in this geotechnical context.

proposals.title: Direct Observation of Hydro-Mechanical Coupling in Slopes: Linking Perched Water Table Dynamics to Progressive Failure via Distributed Fiber Optic Sensing
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced landslides are a major global hazard, yet the precise spatiotemporal link between subsurface water dynamics (e.g., perched water table formation) and the initiation of slope deformation remains poorly observed. Current models, often validated with point sensors, lack the continuous spatial resolution to capture the localized onset and progression of failure, limiting predictive capability. This gap is critical for slopes underlain by a soil-bedrock interface, a common failure plane.
proposals.hypothesis: The rise of a perched water table at a soil-bedrock interface triggers a distinct, spatially localized strain response that precedes macroscopic failure, and the sequence of progressive rupture can be directly mapped and temporally linked to the evolving pore pressure field.
proposals.method: We will conduct centrifuge experiments on instrumented sand-over-rigid-base slope models. Distributed Fiber Optic Sensing (DFOS) cables, using Optical Frequency Domain Reflectometry (OFDR) for high spatial resolution (<1 cm), will be embedded within the soil mass and along the interface to capture continuous strain profiles. A controlled rainfall simulator will induce perched water table formation, with pore pressure transducers providing concurrent hydrological data. The coupled hydro-mechanical response will be analyzed using a finite element framework (e.g., OpenGeoSys) that incorporates Biot-type poroelasticity and the relative fluid-solid acceleration, with the DFOS strain data used for rigorous model validation and parameter calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide an unprecedented experimental dataset directly correlating high-resolution strain evolution with perched water dynamics. It will yield a validated, high-fidelity hydro-mechanical model capable of simulating the pre-failure deformation sequence. The findings will advance the fundamental understanding of failure initiation in unsaturated/saturated slopes and improve early warning criteria.
proposals.novelty: The integration of DFOS directly into physical models to obtain continuous, high-resolution strain maps of the entire soil mass and critical interface during perched aquifer evolution is a significant advance over discrete instrumentation. This allows for the first direct, spatiotemporally explicit validation of coupled hydro-mechanical models for progressive slope failure.

proposals.title: A Coupled Hydro-Mechanical Prognostic Model for Forecasting Time-to-Failure of Slopes under Dynamic Rainfall Infiltration
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced landslides are a major global hazard, but current early warning systems often rely on empirical rainfall thresholds or simplified stability models that lack a direct, physics-based link between transient pore-water pressure dynamics and the progressive mechanical failure of the slope. This limits the ability to provide actionable, mechanism-specific forecasts of remaining useful life for critical slopes and embankments. A prognostic framework that integrates high-resolution groundwater data with soil mechanics is urgently needed to move from generic hazard alerts to quantifiable time-to-failure predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly coupling the dynamics of transient, three-dimensional groundwater flow—including relative fluid acceleration effects—with a critical-state soil constitutive model will enable accurate, probabilistic forecasting of the time-to-failure for slopes undergoing retrogressive failure under dynamic hydrologic forcing.
proposals.method: We will develop a coupled finite element model using a modified u-p-U formulation to capture the dynamics of partially saturated poroelastic media, incorporating the relative acceleration of the fluid phase as in the researcher's prior work. The model will be initialized with field data (e.g., soil properties, slope geometry) and forced with high-resolution, time-varying rainfall infiltration boundary conditions. The mechanical component will implement a strain-softening constitutive model to simulate progressive failure. A Bayesian updating algorithm (e.g., particle filter) will assimilate real-time pore-pressure and displacement monitoring data to continuously calibrate model parameters and generate probabilistic forecasts of time-to-failure.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source computational framework for physics-based prognosis of geotechnical infrastructure. It will advance the field by providing a validated methodology to translate real-time groundwater monitoring data into probabilistic failure-time forecasts with quantified uncertainty, directly supporting risk-based decision-making for slope management.
proposals.novelty: While coupled hydro-mechanical models exist, their application as real-time, data-assimilating prognostic tools for generating probabilistic time-to-failure forecasts under dynamic groundwater forcing is novel. This integrates the researcher's expertise in dynamic poroelasticity, infiltration in slopes, and soil liquefaction behavior into a unified prognostic framework.

proposals.title: A Physics-Grounded Upscaling Framework for Slope Failure Acceleration: From Suction Loss in Element Tests to System-Scale Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced slope failures are a major global hazard, yet current predictive models often rely on empirical thresholds or simplified stability factors, failing to capture the critical dynamics of failure acceleration and extent. The complex interplay between infiltration-driven suction loss, soil fabric evolution, and pre-existing groundwater conditions, especially on slopes, is not mechanistically represented in most large-scale simulations. This gap limits our ability to forecast not just the initiation, but the speed and runout of failures, which is essential for early warning.
proposals.hypothesis: The acceleration dynamics of a failing slope can be predicted by explicitly upscaling a constitutive model that captures the coupled hydromechanical response of soil elements to transient seepage and infiltration, validated against controlled element-scale tests.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a suite of advanced, instrumented triaxial and shear tests on unsaturated residual sand under combined hydraulic gradients and mechanical loading to define a new constitutive model for suction loss and fabric change. This model will be implemented within a coupled finite element framework (e.g., using OpenSees or a custom code) that solves for solid deformation and pore fluid flow, explicitly incorporating relative fluid acceleration effects. The upscaling will be achieved by using this constitutive model at every integration point in a detailed slope model, enabling the simulation of failure initiation, acceleration, and propagation dynamics under realistic rainfall and groundwater scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, scale-bridging computational tool that moves slope stability analysis from a binary safe/fail assessment to a predictive science of failure dynamics. It will provide a fundamental link between material-scale hydromechanical processes and system-scale geohazard behavior. The resulting framework will offer a more mechanistic basis for assessing landslide hazard intensity (velocity, depth) for risk quantification.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, physics-based upscaling of a constitutive model derived from controlled element-scale infiltration-seepage tests to directly predict system-scale failure acceleration dynamics, rather than just initiation. This fusion of high-precision element testing and dynamics-focused system modeling for unsaturated slopes is a significant advance beyond conventional limit equilibrium or decoupled seepage-stability analyses.

proposals.title: Field-Calibrated Dynamic Slope Stability: Fusing Time-Lapse Electrical Resistivity Tomography with a Poroelastic Infinite Slope Model for Rainfall-Induced Failure Prediction
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced landslides remain a major global hazard, but predictive models are limited by an inability to capture the transient, spatially heterogeneous pore pressure fields that drive failure. Current infinite slope models often use simplified, steady-state infiltration assumptions, while advanced coupled hydro-mechanical models are rarely validated against field-scale, temporally dense pore pressure data. This gap hinders the development of reliable, physics-based early warning systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That a dynamic infinite slope model, explicitly incorporating the spatiotemporal evolution of pore pressure from infiltration, can be directly calibrated and validated using field-scale geophysical monitoring data, leading to a significantly improved predictive capability for rainfall-triggered slope instability.
proposals.method: We will deploy a permanent array of surface electrodes for time-lapse 2D/3D Electrical Resistivity Tomography (ERT) on a well-instrumented natural slope analogue to monitor rainfall-induced wetting fronts and pore pressure changes, validated by co-located piezometers. This field data will drive the calibration of a coupled finite element model, building on the PI's prior work, that solves Richards' equation for variably saturated flow in a sloping porous medium coupled with a dynamic infinite slope stability analysis. The model will be inverted using a Bayesian framework to constrain hydraulic parameters (e.g., saturated hydraulic conductivity, van Genuchten parameters) and directly output a time-varying factor of safety.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, mechanistic framework that translates real-time geophysical signatures into a dynamic slope stability assessment. It will provide a novel dataset linking distributed pore pressure evolution to slope response, and deliver a calibrated model that can be adapted for early warning by integrating rainfall forecasts.
proposals.novelty: The direct, mechanistic fusion of field-scale geophysical monitoring (ERT) for continuous pore pressure field estimation with a dynamic infinite slope model calibration is a significant advance over common practice, moving beyond point measurements and steady-state assumptions to a fully transient, spatially explicit predictive framework.

proposals.title: Field Validation of Centrifuge-Derived Rain-Induced Pore Pressure Dynamics in Slopes via Coupled Geophysical Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced landslides are a major global hazard, often triggered by complex interactions between infiltration, slope geometry, and soil properties that alter pore pressures and reduce stability. While centrifuge modelling, such as Uzuoka's work on sand reliquefaction on slopes, provides controlled insights into these dynamics, a critical gap exists in validating these physical model findings at the natural field scale under real, transient rainfall conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: That time-lapse seismic refraction tomography (SRT) and electrical resistivity tomography (ERT) can quantitatively capture the spatiotemporal evolution of saturation and pore pressure in a sandy slope analogue during rainfall, and that these field measurements will validate the patterns of groundwater interaction and instability thresholds predicted by prior centrifuge experiments.
proposals.method: We will instrument a natural, unsaturated sandy slope (a field analogue) with a permanent array of geophones and electrodes. Time-lapse ERT, using a Wenner-Schlumberger array and inverse modelling with ResIPy, will map moisture content changes. Concurrently, active-source SRT, processed with a tomographic inversion algorithm like PStomo_eq, will monitor P-wave velocity variations linked to saturation. Data will be integrated into a coupled hydro-geophysical framework, correlating resistivity and seismic velocity with pore pressure changes, and directly compared to the centrifuge data on rainfall infiltration and pore pressure response in sloping sand.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rare, direct field validation dataset for centrifuge models of slope hydrology, strengthening the physical basis for using centrifuge results in hazard assessment. It will also demonstrate a practical, coupled geophysical methodology for non-invasive monitoring of slope stability precursors in sandy soils.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted use of field geophysical monitoring not just for detection, but for the explicit, quantitative validation of centrifuge-physical-model findings on rain-groundwater dynamics, creating a crucial feedback loop between controlled experimentation and real-world complexity.

proposals.title: A Geo-Fluidic Element-Scale Testing Platform for High-Resolution Hydromechanical Constitutive Model Calibration
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced slope failures, a major geohazard, are driven by complex, coupled hydromechanical processes where infiltration and seepage reduce soil suction and alter effective stress. Existing constitutive models for unsaturated soils are often calibrated with data from separate, macro-scale tests that cannot independently control all driving factors, leading to significant uncertainty in stability predictions for slopes and embankments under dynamic conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting microfluidic principles to create a controlled 'geo-fluidic' testing environment, we can precisely isolate and quantify the independent and interactive contributions of suction loss, hydraulic gradient, and fluid chemistry to the constitutive response of a soil element, enabling the definition of a more robust, physically-based constitutive model.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a microfluidic-inspired test cell using transparent polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) or glass, integrating micro-pore pressure transducers and a high-resolution optical system for particle image velocimetry (PIV). The device will allow independent control of matric suction (via axis translation), hydraulic gradient (via precise syringe pumps), and pore fluid chemistry. A coupled finite element analysis framework, based on Uzuoka's previous work on poroelastic media dynamics, will be used to simulate the experimental boundary conditions and interpret the high-resolution displacement and flow field data to back-calculate stress-strain relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-fidelity, element-scale experimental dataset on soil response to simultaneous suction and seepage forces. It will deliver a directly calibrated constitutive modeling framework that explicitly accounts for these coupled drivers, significantly improving the predictive accuracy of numerical stability analyses for natural slopes and engineered embankments under rainfall and seismic shaking.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in geomechanics for its direct adaptation of biomedical microfluidic assay concepts to achieve unprecedented independent control and measurement of coupled hydromechanical drivers at the soil element scale, moving beyond traditional triaxial or infiltration column apparatuses.

proposals.title: A Constitutive Framework for Suction Loss in Unsaturated Sands Under Combined Seepage and Infiltration: Element-Scale Hydromechanical Testing and Model Calibration
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced slope failures in unsaturated sandy soils are a major geohazard, often triggered by the loss of matric suction during infiltration. Current predictive models are limited because they typically treat seepage (steady-state flow) and transient infiltration separately, failing to capture their complex, coupled hydromechanical interactions that govern the true onset of instability. A precise, physics-based constitutive model for suction loss under these combined flow conditions is critically needed to improve the assessment of slope stability under dynamic rainfall patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: The effective stress path and the resulting degradation of shear strength in an unsaturated sand element subjected to coupled seepage and infiltration can be uniquely defined by a constitutive model that explicitly incorporates the relative acceleration of the pore fluid and the evolving hydraulic boundary conditions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of controlled, element-scale tests using a modified stress-path triaxial apparatus equipped with high-suction tensiometers and pore pressure transducers. Unsaturated sand specimens will be subjected to predefined initial seepage gradients, followed by simulated infiltration events using a precision pump system. The dynamic response (stress, strain, suction, water content) will be measured. The data will be used to calibrate and extend a coupled finite element poroelasticity framework, similar to Uzuoka's prior work, by implementing a new constitutive law within the code that links the relative fluid acceleration term to the rate of suction loss and volumetric deformation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a calibrated, element-scale constitutive model for suction loss in sands under combined flow, providing the fundamental building block for more accurate slope-scale stability simulations. It will directly inform the parameterization of advanced numerical models for rainfall-induced landslides and soil liquefaction, bridging the gap between laboratory element behavior and field-scale performance.
proposals.novelty: While infiltration and seepage are studied in isolation, this proposal uniquely focuses on their *combined* mechanical effect at the constitutive level, explicitly integrating the dynamic pore fluid response into the failure criterion for unsaturated sands. The experimental protocol to precisely control and measure both flow regimes simultaneously in a single element test is a novel advancement.

proposals.title: Inverting Landslide Scars to Reconstruct Pre-Failure Hydrological Conditions: A Coupled Geomechanical and Seismic-Inspired Inverse Framework
proposals.motivation: Quantifying the hydrological triggers (e.g., groundwater table, rainfall history) of past landslides is critical for hazard assessment but remains a major challenge, as pre-failure data is rarely available. Existing paleo-hydrological methods are often qualitative. This gap limits our ability to calibrate regional hydrogeological models for slope stability under extreme climatic conditions, a pressing need in the context of climate change and increased rainfall variability observed pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: The geometry and sedimentological features of a landslide scar contain a unique signature of the pre-failure pore pressure distribution and its temporal evolution. This signature can be quantitatively inverted using a calibrated geomechanical model, analogous to how seismic waveforms are inverted for source parameters.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-step numerical framework. First, a forward model will couple a finite element analysis of unsaturated-saturated flow (using Richards' equation) with a poro-elastoplastic slope stability model, building on the researcher's experience with coupled finite element analysis of poroelastic media. Second, we will implement a Bayesian inversion algorithm (e.g., Markov Chain Monte Carlo) to iteratively adjust the unknown initial groundwater table and transient rainfall boundary conditions. The misfit function will compare simulated failure geometry (scar depth, length) and mobilized material with high-resolution LiDAR and field data from a well-documented landslide.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative tool to reconstruct the hydrological forcing of past landslides, moving beyond qualitative assessments. It will yield probabilistic estimates of pre-failure pore pressure states, directly informing the calibration of infiltration models for residual soils and the assessment of reliquefaction potential in slopes. The methodology bridges seismological inverse theory and geotechnical engineering.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in explicitly treating the landslide scar as the observable output of an inverse problem to reconstruct its hydrological cause, a concept not previously applied in geotechnical practice. It uniquely integrates the researcher's expertise in infiltration testing, centrifuge modeling of sloping ground, and coupled poroelastic dynamics into a single inverse analysis framework.

proposals.title: Optimizing Subsurface Drainage for Slope Stabilization: A Bio-Inspired 'Soil Stent' Approach Using Centrifuge and Poroelastic Analysis
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced slope failures, often triggered by rising pore water pressure, remain a major geohazard. Current drainage design is largely empirical, lacking a systematic framework to optimize drain geometry for dynamic pore pressure management during seismic or intense rainfall events. This gap limits the long-term reliability and resilience of stabilization measures in residual soils and liquefiable sands.
proposals.hypothesis: Treating subsurface drains as 'soil stents' and systematically varying their configuration (e.g., spacing, orientation, permeability contrast) will reveal optimal geometries that most effectively control the pore pressure field and seepage forces, thereby maximizing slope stability under simulated failure conditions.
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-pronged approach. First, a series of centrifuge model tests on instrumented sand and residual soil slopes will be conducted, simulating rainfall and seismic shaking. Different drain configurations (e.g., horizontal, vertical, dendritic networks) will be embedded and their performance in managing excess pore pressure and preventing liquefaction or failure will be quantified. Second, these physical models will be simulated using coupled finite element analysis of poroelastic media, incorporating relative fluid acceleration, to back-analyze the full 3D pore pressure response and seepage forces, enabling parametric optimization of drain geometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physics-based methodology for deriving optimized drainage designs rather than merely verifying them. It will yield specific design guidelines linking drain geometry to pore pressure dissipation efficiency in different soil types and slope conditions, directly enhancing predictive capability for slope stabilization.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the conceptual analogy to vascular stents, framing drainage design as an active optimization problem for managing a dynamic fluid (pore water) field. This shifts the focus from passive drainage to the engineered, geometry-dependent performance of the drain network itself under transient loads.

proposals.title: Coupled Numerical and Centrifuge Analysis of Pile Foundation Response Under Combined Seismic, Rainfall, and Wind-Wave Loading on Sloping Coastal Ground
proposals.motivation: Critical infrastructure in coastal and hillslope regions is increasingly exposed to multi-hazard scenarios, such as an earthquake occurring during a typhoon or monsoon season, where intense rainfall and storm-induced wave/wind loads act simultaneously. Pre-2026 design codes and research often treat these hazards independently, potentially underestimating the coupled effects of dynamic shaking, pore pressure generation from cyclic environmental loads, and altered soil strength on sloping ground.
proposals.hypothesis: The superposition of long-duration, low-frequency environmental cyclic loads (from wind/waves and antecedent rainfall infiltration) with high-frequency seismic shaking will significantly amplify excess pore water pressure generation in sandy slopes, leading to greater foundation displacement and a higher risk of soil liquefaction or slope failure than predicted by analyzing the hazards separately.
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-pronged approach: (1) Advanced centrifuge modelling to physically simulate combined loading on instrumented pile groups in sloping sand, using actuators to apply seismic base shaking synchronized with surface loading to simulate wind/wave forces and a rainfall simulator to control infiltration. (2) Coupled finite element analysis using a u-p-U formulation (considering solid displacement, pore fluid pressure, and relative fluid acceleration) to numerically model the dynamics of the unsaturated-saturated poroelastic system. The numerical model will be calibrated and validated against centrifuge data, then used for extensive parametric studies on slope angle, load phasing, and soil saturation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, experimentally validated framework for assessing foundation performance under realistic multi-hazard conditions. It will provide new insights into the critical phasing and intensity relationships between different load types and deliver practical guidelines for the performance-based design of foundations in vulnerable coastal and hillslope environments.
proposals.novelty: While Uzuoka's prior work examined seismic loading on slopes and reliquefaction, this proposal uniquely integrates antecedent and concurrent environmental cyclic loads (wind/wave and rainfall infiltration) with seismic shaking in a single experimental-numerical framework, explicitly addressing their synergistic impact on soil-fluid-structure interaction.

proposals.title: A Coupled Effective Stress Framework for Seismic Soil-Structure-Fluid Interaction: Hydrodynamic Pressures and Seabed Pore Pressure Response
proposals.motivation: The seismic performance of offshore and nearshore structures, such as piles and quay walls, is critically governed by the coupled interaction between soil skeleton, pore fluid, and the surrounding water body. Pre-2026 design practices often simplify hydrodynamic pressures or decouple seabed liquefaction analysis from the full fluid-structure interaction, potentially underestimating the dynamic loads and excess pore pressures that drive failure during events like soil reliquefaction.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly incorporating hydrodynamic pressure waves and their interaction with a deforming, partially saturated seabed will significantly alter the computed dynamic effective stress path and liquefaction potential around a structure compared to analyses that treat these phenomena in isolation.
proposals.method: We will extend the researcher's existing coupled finite element framework for poroelastic media (u-p-U form) to include a surface fluid domain governed by the acoustic wave equation, coupled at the seabed interface via continuity of pressure and velocity. The soil domain will implement a critical state-based constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand) to capture cyclic liquefaction. The coupled system will be solved using a partitioned iterative scheme (e.g., a staggered Gauss-Seidel approach) within an open-source finite element platform like OpenSees or by extending in-house code. Validation will use centrifuge test data from prior work on pile groups in sloping sand under dynamic excitation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated numerical tool for integrated seismic risk assessment of coastal geotechnical systems. It will quantify the feedback between hydrodynamic loading, seabed pore pressure generation, and structural response, leading to more accurate fragility estimates. The results will inform guidelines for when full coupled analysis is essential for design.
proposals.novelty: While fluid-structure and soil-structure interaction are studied separately, their explicit three-way coupling within a dynamic effective stress framework for sloping, liquefiable seabeds is a significant advancement. This moves beyond treating hydrodynamic pressure as a simple added mass or analyzing seabed response with rigid-boundary water assumptions.

proposals.title: Inversion of Foundation Response Records for In-Situ Soil State Diagnosis: A Coupled Poroelastic Framework for Sloping Ground
proposals.motivation: Post-earthquake site characterization is critical for assessing liquefaction risk and residual slope stability, but traditional in-situ tests are often impractical after a major event. The measured dynamic response of existing foundations provides a unique, but underutilized, data stream that encodes the in-situ soil state during shaking. Leveraging this data could enable rapid, remote diagnosis of soil properties and pore pressure generation, improving post-event safety assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The coupled acceleration time histories of a foundation and the underlying soil column during an earthquake can be inverted using a poroelastic finite element model to estimate key in-situ state parameters, including relative density, initial shear modulus, and permeability, particularly for sloping sand deposits where drainage conditions are complex.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational framework combining a coupled u-p (displacement-pore pressure) finite element formulation, which accounts for relative fluid acceleration as in the researcher's prior work, with a Bayesian inversion algorithm. The forward model will simulate the dynamic soil-foundation interaction on a slope, incorporating varying infiltration conditions to model partial saturation. The inversion will use recorded foundation motions as input to condition the Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling of the posterior distribution of soil parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel methodology for non-destructive, post-event soil state diagnosis. It will advance the understanding of how slope geometry and transient infiltration affect the inverse problem's solvability. The resulting open-source inversion tool will provide a practical link between structural monitoring and geotechnical hazard evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While system identification of soil-structure systems exists, the explicit inversion for fundamental soil state parameters (e.g., relative density) from foundation response using a fully coupled poroelastic model, especially for sloping ground with partial saturation, is a significant and unexplored step. This integrates the researcher's expertise in slopes, sand reliquefaction, and poroelastic dynamics into a single inverse analysis framework.

proposals.title: Coupled Hydromechanical Response of Heterogeneous Slopes to Rainfall and Seismic Loading: A Numerical and Centrifuge Study
proposals.motivation: Slope stability analyses often assume homogeneous soil properties, yet natural slopes are characterized by layered stratigraphy and spatially variable hydraulic conductivity. This simplification fails to capture complex preferential flow paths and localized pore pressure generation, which are critical triggers for rainfall-induced landslides and seismic reliquefaction. A mechanistic understanding of how heterogeneity controls the coupled dynamic response is essential for improved hazard assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial variability of hydraulic properties, particularly in layered slopes, creates distinct patterns of pore-water pressure redistribution and shear strain localization under combined rainfall infiltration and seismic shaking, fundamentally altering the failure mechanism compared to homogeneous assumptions.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled finite element analysis using a u-p-U formulation (solid displacement, pore pressure, fluid displacement) to account for relative fluid acceleration, crucial for dynamic problems. The model will incorporate spatially random fields of saturated hydraulic conductivity and soil-water characteristic curves, generated using Karhunen-Loève expansion. This numerical framework will be validated against a series of centrifuge experiments on instrumented, layered sand-silt slopes subjected to simultaneous rainfall simulation and seismic base shaking.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated numerical framework for analyzing slope stability under multi-hazard scenarios in realistic, heterogeneous soils. It will deliver new insights into the interaction between infiltration patterns, dynamic pore pressure response, and failure initiation, moving beyond the limiting homogeneous slope paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have examined heterogeneity or coupled dynamics separately, this work uniquely integrates spatially variable hydraulic properties into a fully coupled dynamic poroelastic analysis and validates it with physical modelling for combined seismic and rainfall loading, a non-trivial multi-physics problem.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Influence of Intermittent Rainfall Patterns on Slope Stability Across Distinct Soil Textural Classes
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced landslides are a major geohazard, and climate projections (pre-2026) suggest an increase in the frequency of extreme, intermittent rainfall events. Current slope stability models often use simplified, constant-intensity rainfall inputs, which may not capture the critical pore pressure dynamics generated by realistic on-off precipitation cycles. Understanding how these patterns interact with fundamental soil properties is essential for improved hazard assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The destabilizing effect of intermittent rainfall patterns on slope stability is not uniform but is strongly modulated by soil texture, with fine-grained soils (clays) showing a more pronounced and delayed response compared to coarse-grained soils (sands) due to differences in infiltration capacity, unsaturated hydraulic conductivity, and pore pressure dissipation rates.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled numerical modeling framework, building on the researcher's prior work with finite element analysis of poroelastic media. A validated hydromechanical model (e.g., using COMSOL Multiphysics or a custom FE code) will be used to simulate 1D infiltration and slope response under various synthetic intermittent rainfall patterns. The core experimental design is a full-factorial numerical simulation, varying: (1) soil type (clay, silt, loam, silty sand, sand), defined by pre-2026 SWCC and permeability models (e.g., van Genuchten, Brooks-Corey); (2) rainfall pattern (intensity, duration, and inter-storm dry period). The primary response metrics will be the evolution of pore-water pressure, factor of safety over time, and the time to failure.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a systematic classification of soil-specific vulnerability to intermittent rainfall, moving beyond generic rainfall thresholds. It will deliver calibrated, pattern-dependent infiltration parameters for different soil classes that can be integrated into regional slope stability models. The findings will directly inform the design of more effective early warning systems that account for both forecasted rainfall patterns and local soil maps.
proposals.novelty: While the separate effects of rainfall and soil are well-studied, the explicit, coupled investigation of how *temporal rainfall patterns* differentially impact a *spectrum of soil textural classes* using advanced poroelastic dynamics is novel. This integrates the researcher's expertise in infiltration measurement, soil dynamics, and coupled numerical analysis to address a clear gap in geotechnical hazard modeling.

proposals.title: Spectral Characterization of Rainfall-Induced Pore Pressure Dynamics in Sloping Residual Soils
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced landslides are a major geohazard, yet the transient hydraulic response of unsaturated, sloping soils to complex rainfall patterns remains poorly quantified. Current slope stability models often rely on simplified infiltration assumptions, failing to capture the frequency-dependent nature of pore pressure build-up and dissipation, which is critical for predicting failure timing. Advanced signal processing of pore pressure data offers a pathway to directly link rainfall forcing to subsurface hydraulic response.
proposals.hypothesis: The frequency content of a rainfall time series, characterized via spectral analysis, governs the dominant modes of pore pressure response in a sloping residual soil profile, with low-frequency components driving deep-seated pressure changes and high-frequency components affecting near-surface dynamics.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled centrifuge experiments on instrumented, sloping models of residual sand, applying synthetic rainfall patterns with varying intensity-duration-frequency characteristics. Pore pressure transducer data will be analyzed using both Fourier Transform (to identify dominant response frequencies) and Continuous Wavelet Transform (specifically the Morlet wavelet) to resolve time-frequency localization of transient pressure signals. The transfer function between rainfall input and pore pressure response at different depths will be computed to quantify system damping and phase lag.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physics-informed framework for interpreting in-situ pore pressure monitoring data by decomposing it into rainfall-forced and noise components. It will yield quantitative metrics (e.g., characteristic response frequencies, damping ratios) for calibrating and validating coupled hydro-mechanical finite element models, like those used in the researcher's prior work, leading to improved early warning criteria.
proposals.novelty: While signal processing is common in seismology, its application to characterize the hydraulic response of unsaturated, sloping soils to rainfall is novel. This approach moves beyond traditional time-domain infiltration models to a frequency-domain analysis, explicitly linking the spectral signature of the trigger to the soil's internal response dynamics.

proposals.title: Pore-Scale Visualization of Intermittent Rainfall Infiltration and Its Impact on Preferential Flow Path Evolution in Sloping Sands
proposals.motivation: Rainfall-induced slope failures are a major geohazard, yet current macro-scale infiltration models often fail to predict the onset of instability accurately. This discrepancy is partly due to an incomplete understanding of how intermittent wetting cycles, characteristic of natural rainfall, dynamically alter the soil fabric at the pore scale, creating or reinforcing preferential flow paths that control pore pressure response and slope stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intermittent wetting fronts, as opposed to steady infiltration, will cause significant, non-reversible rearrangement of sand grains and pore structures in sloping conditions, leading to the progressive development of preferential flow networks that dominate subsequent hydrological response and increase reliquefaction susceptibility.
proposals.method: We will use synchrotron-based micro-CT imaging to perform time-lapse, 4D visualization of a sloping sand specimen subjected to controlled, intermittent infiltration in a custom-designed miniature flow cell. The 3D image sequences will be processed using watershed segmentation algorithms (e.g., in ImageJ/Fiji) to quantify pore network geometry and particle displacement. These pore-scale dynamics will be directly linked to macro-scale hydraulic response measured via pressure sensors, and the data will inform the calibration of a coupled finite element model (using OpenGeoSys or similar) that incorporates relative fluid acceleration effects to upscale the observed fabric evolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, 4D observational evidence of how intermittent rainfall drives pore-scale fabric change in sands, establishing a mechanistic link between wetting history and the emergence of preferential flow. It will yield a novel, microstructure-informed framework for improving the prediction of infiltration dynamics and instability in slopes, directly relevant to landslide early warning systems.
proposals.novelty: While micro-CT has been used for static soil imaging, its application to dynamically capture fabric evolution during intermittent, slope-driven infiltration is novel. This bridges the critical gap between Uzuoka's prior work on macro-scale infiltration response, slope reliquefaction, and advanced poroelastic modeling.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Seismic and Hydrological Response of Embankments with Spatially Variable Fill Properties: A Coupled Centrifuge and Numerical Study
proposals.motivation: Geotechnical design of earth embankments for slopes and levees often assumes homogeneous fill properties for simplicity, despite natural and compacted soils exhibiting spatial variability and layering. This assumption is challenged during combined seismic shaking and rainfall infiltration, which can trigger localized failures. Pre-2026 research by Uzuoka and others highlights the critical role of coupled hydromechanical processes in slope stability, yet the impact of realistic, non-uniform soil property distributions on system response remains poorly quantified.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic performance and post-shaking infiltration behavior of an embankment are governed not just by average soil properties but by the spatial structure (e.g., layers, lenses, random fields) of permeability and shear strength, leading to failure mechanisms and pore pressure dynamics distinct from those predicted by homogeneous models.
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-pronged approach: 1) Centrifuge physical modelling of scaled embankments constructed with controlled spatial variability (e.g., layered sand-clay systems or fill with embedded low-permeability lenses), subjected to sequential shaking and rainfall. 2) Coupled finite element numerical simulation using a code like COMSOL or an in-house implementation of Biot's equations with relative fluid acceleration (extending Uzuoka's prior work). The numerical domain will incorporate spatially random fields of permeability and shear modulus, generated using the Karhunen-Loève expansion, to statistically analyze the system response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated framework for assessing embankment vulnerability under multi-hazard scenarios that accounts for inherent spatial variability. It will deliver practical guidelines on when simplified homogeneous assumptions are non-conservative and advance the field of performance-based geotechnical earthquake engineering for earth structures.
proposals.novelty: While variability in natural deposits is acknowledged, this study uniquely combines advanced centrifuge testing with stochastic coupled hydromechanical simulation specifically for engineered fill, targeting the interplay between pre-seismic layering, shaking-induced redistribution, and post-seismic infiltration—a sequence critical for long-term performance.

proposals.title: Resonant Pore Pressure Buildup: A Coupled Dynamic-Seepage Analysis of Post-Earthquake Slope Stability Under Steady-State Seepage
proposals.motivation: Post-earthquake slope failures often occur hours or days after shaking, suggesting a critical role of post-seismic pore pressure redistribution. Current practice assumes rapid drawdown is the most critical seepage condition, potentially underestimating the hazard from post-earthquake steady-state seepage. This research addresses a critical gap in understanding how specific seismic shaking patterns precondition a slope, making subsequent rainfall infiltration uniquely destabilizing.
proposals.hypothesis: Seismic shaking with frequency content and duration that resonates with the natural drainage periods of a soil slope can induce a progressive, resonant buildup of pore pressures, creating a post-earthquake steady-state seepage condition more critical for slope stability than classical rapid drawdown.
proposals.method: The research will employ a coupled finite element analysis of the dynamics of poroelastic media, building on Uzuoka's prior work. We will implement the u-p-U formulation to capture relative fluid acceleration. A parametric study using centrifuge-model-validated input motions will identify critical shaking patterns. The analysis will systematically vary seismic frequency content, duration, and sequencing against soil hydraulic conductivity and slope geometry to map conditions leading to resonant pore pressure amplification. Post-shaking, a steady-state seepage analysis will be performed to compute the resultant factor of safety.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for assessing post-earthquake slope stability that integrates dynamic loading characteristics with long-term seepage. It will yield guidelines for identifying 'resonant' seismic signatures that pose the greatest delayed hazard. The results could fundamentally shift the critical condition considered in the design of slopes in seismically active, rainfall-prone regions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the structural dynamics concept of resonance and cumulative damage to the coupled hydro-mechanical problem of seismic pore pressure generation and dissipation. It challenges the entrenched geotechnical assumption that rapid drawdown is unconditionally the most critical seepage condition.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Mapping of Critical Drawdown Rates in Slopes via PIV-Tracked Fabric Evolution
proposals.motivation: Rapid drawdown of water levels in slopes, whether due to reservoir operation or tidal/rainfall events, is a major trigger for instability and landslides. Current predictive models rely on bulk effective stress or pore pressure thresholds, which fail to capture the internal kinematic processes leading to failure. A mechanistic understanding linking drawdown rate to the evolution of soil fabric is critically needed to improve the safety and resilience of geotechnical infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: The critical drawdown rate for slope failure is governed by a specific, observable pattern of internal soil fabric re-organization and kinematic instability, which precedes macroscopic collapse and can be quantified via full-field displacement analysis.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of physical model tests in a transparent-sided flume using fused quartz or hydrogel soil analogs, enabling optical access. A programmable weir will impose a spectrum of controlled drawdown rates. For each test, high-resolution Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) will track the full-field, two-dimensional displacement vectors of the soil fabric in real-time. The PIV analysis will utilize a multi-pass, iterative cross-correlation algorithm (e.g., using open-source software like PIVlab or OpenPIV) to compute incremental and cumulative strain fields, identifying localized shear bands and internal erosion patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind dataset directly correlating drawdown rate with the spatiotemporal evolution of soil fabric kinematics. It will advance slope stability theory by providing a physically-based, micro-mechanical criterion for critical drawdown, moving beyond phenomenological limits. The findings will directly inform the design of safer drawdown protocols for embankment dams and natural slopes.
proposals.novelty: While PIV has been used in geotechnics, its application to map the complete internal kinematic response to a *spectrum* of drawdown rates, explicitly linking fabric evolution to a critical threshold, is novel. This fusion of controlled hydraulic loading with high-resolution fabric tracking creates a new pathway for mechanistic model development.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Classification of Rainfall- and Seismic-Induced Slope Failures via Distributed Fiber Optic Sensing in Centrifuge Models
proposals.motivation: Slope failures are triggered by complex interactions between rainfall infiltration, seismic shaking, and soil type, yet current predictive models often treat soil as a generic material. This leads to inaccurate assessments of failure initiation and progression, particularly for intermediate soils like silts or residual soils. A data-driven, soil-specific framework is needed to link intrinsic soil properties directly to observed failure mechanisms for improved hazard prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: The initiation, localization, and propagation of failure in slopes under dynamic or hydrologic loading are fundamentally controlled by a soil's grain size distribution, plasticity, and permeability, leading to distinct, classifiable strain signatures measurable by distributed fiber optic sensing (DFOS).
proposals.method: We will construct dynamic centrifuge models of 1:40 scale embankments using a soil matrix: clean sand, low-plasticity silt, and cohesive clay. Each model will be instrumented with a dense, multi-axis DFOS array (using pre-2026 Rayleigh or Brillouin scattering techniques) embedded within the soil mass. Models will be subjected to sequential tests: (1) staged rainfall simulation via a sprinkler system to induce infiltration-driven failure, and (2) seismic shaking via an in-flight actuator to trigger liquefaction or inertial failure. The high-resolution, continuous strain and temperature data will be processed using existing algorithms (e.g., COMSOL Multiphysics coupled finite element analysis for pore pressure-strain validation) to extract deformation patterns.
