proposals.contribution: This work will produce a first-of-its-kind dataset correlating full-field, high-resolution strain evolution with soil type and triggering condition. It will yield a mechanistic classification chart linking soil properties to failure modes (e.g., retrogressive cracking vs. basal liquefaction). The validated data will directly improve the calibration of advanced constitutive models in numerical simulations for slope stability.
proposals.novelty: While DFOS has been used in geotechnical monitoring, its systematic application within a controlled centrifuge environment to deconvolve the coupled effects of soil type, infiltration, and shaking on failure mechanism classification is novel. The strategic soil matrix moves beyond binary sand/clay studies to address critical intermediate soils.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Centrifuge-Numerical Framework for Stress-Controlled Embankment Breach Initiation and Debris Flow Runout Prediction
proposals.motivation: Predicting the inundation zone of a landslide or embankment breach is critical for risk assessment, but current methods are hampered by poor constraints on the initial failure volume and material state. Runout models are often initialized with arbitrary assumptions, leading to large uncertainties in deposition forecasts. A robust, physics-based link between the geotechnically-controlled failure initiation and the subsequent fluid dynamics of flow is therefore a major pre-2026 gap in hazard analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: That centrifuge modeling of stress-controlled embankment breach can provide precise, physically-consistent initial conditions (volume, density, saturation, and momentum) for depth-averaged debris flow models, and that this coupled framework will significantly improve the accuracy of long-travel-runout predictions across complex topography.
proposals.method: Centrifuge tests on model embankments (sandy slopes) will induce failure under controlled, prototype-scale stresses, with high-speed imaging and PIV analysis to quantify the initial kinematics and mobilized volume. The output—a characterized, fast-moving mass—will directly initialize a depth-averaged numerical model (e.g., Dan3D or r.avaflow) by specifying the initial release zone geometry and momentum. The numerical model will be calibrated against the centrifuge-measured proximal runout, then applied to high-resolution digital terrain models to simulate distal deposition.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, end-to-end methodology for debris flow hazard mapping that rigorously connects failure mechanics to runout dynamics. It will deliver a new benchmark dataset for model validation and provide practitioners with a calibrated framework for predicting inundation zones with reduced uncertainty, directly supporting infrastructure resilience and land-use planning.
proposals.novelty: The explicit, experimental control and measurement of the stress state and kinematics at the moment of breach initiation to define numerical model inputs is a novel integration of geotechnical centrifuge modeling and geophysical mass flow simulation, moving beyond separate treatment of 'failure' and 'runout'.

proposals.title: Inverse Reconstruction of Pre-Failure Hydrological Conditions from 3D Crack Network Tomography in Slope Failure Samples
proposals.motivation: The precise hydrological conditions leading to rainfall-induced landslides remain poorly constrained, as pore pressure and suction are rarely measured in-situ at the scale of the failing mass. This limits the predictive capability of coupled hydromechanical models. High-resolution 3D imaging of post-failure soil fabric offers a novel, spatially dense data source to infer these missing pre-failure conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The 3D topology and aperture distribution of desiccation or shear-induced crack networks in failed soil samples encode a unique signature of the spatial heterogeneity and temporal evolution of soil suction and pore-water pressure that existed prior to failure.
proposals.method: We will acquire X-ray CT scans of frozen landslide debris and laboratory failure specimens to reconstruct 3D crack networks. Using a coupled finite element model for unsaturated flow and deformation in poroelastic media (extending Uzuoka's prior work), we will implement an inverse modeling framework. The 3D crack geometry will serve as the target state. We will employ gradient-based optimization (e.g., adjoint method) or Bayesian inversion to find the pre-failure infiltration history and initial conditions that, when simulated forward, produce a crack network statistically congruent with the observed tomography.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology to calibrate and validate landslide hydrology models using physical evidence from failures. It will yield quantitative, spatially distributed estimates of pre-failure pore pressure fields that are otherwise unattainable, directly linking infiltration models to mechanical failure patterns.
proposals.novelty: While crack networks are often studied descriptively, using their full 3D topology as a constraint for inverse hydrological modeling of slope failures is a novel fusion of geotechnical imaging and computational inverse theory. This moves beyond correlative relationships to a causative, physics-based inference.

proposals.title: Calibrating Regional Slope Stability Models with Grain-Scale Kinematic Precursors from PIV Experiments
proposals.motivation: Regional landslide early warning systems (LEWS) often rely on empirical rainfall thresholds or static susceptibility maps, which lack a direct physical basis in the pre-failure soil mechanics. This limits their ability to forecast the timing of failure under complex, transient hydro-mechanical conditions, such as those induced by rainfall infiltration on different slopes. A physics-based link between observable grain-scale deformation precursors and regional slope response is critically needed to improve forecast accuracy.
proposals.hypothesis: That high-resolution kinematic parameters (e.g., localized strain rate fields, acceleration of particle movements) derived from laboratory PIV tests under controlled infiltration and shaking can serve as calibrated, physically meaningful precursors. When integrated into a regional GIS-based model, these precursors can dynamically update failure probability forecasts.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of centrifuge and 1-g flume tests on sandy slopes, instrumented with PIV and pore pressure sensors, to simulate rainfall infiltration and seismic shaking. The PIV data will be processed to extract novel precursor metrics (e.g., spatial-temporal evolution of shear strain localization). These metrics will calibrate a coupled hydro-mechanical finite element model (using a formulation considering relative fluid acceleration, extending Uzuoka's prior work). The calibrated model will be implemented within a GIS framework using Python, where regional geospatial data (slope, soil, groundwater) will drive dynamic, cell-by-cell simulations of stability evolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new methodology for deriving physics-based landslide precursors, moving beyond purely empirical correlations. It will deliver a prototype GIS-integrated dynamic model capable of simulating time-evolving stability, directly contributing to the next generation of physics-informed regional LEWS.
proposals.novelty: The direct fusion of grain-scale kinematic data from advanced laboratory PIV with regional-scale GIS modeling to define and implement dynamic, mechanically calibrated precursor parameters is a novel, multi-scale approach not present in existing static susceptibility or purely hydrological warning frameworks.

proposals.title: A Coupled Experimental-Numerical Framework for Predicting Drawdown-Induced Shear Failure in Slopes: Role of Stress Path, Fabric, and Stress Rotation
proposals.motivation: Rapid drawdown of water levels in reservoirs and riverbanks is a critical trigger for slope instability, posing significant geohazards. Existing predictive models often rely on simplified, isotropic soil parameters and fail to capture the complex, anisotropic stress history and fabric evolution induced by coupled infiltration, shaking, and multi-directional stress changes during drawdown. This gap leads to unreliable safety assessments for slopes under dynamic hydrological conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: The anisotropic strength and deformation response of a soil element subjected to drawdown can be accurately characterized by laboratory tests replicating the critical, pre-failure stress path, and this characterization enables a high-fidelity 3D numerical simulation of the complete failure mechanism, including the effects of stress rotation and evolving fabric.
proposals.method: Advanced stress-path-controlled hollow cylinder torsional shear (HCTS) tests will be performed on reconstituted sand and residual soil specimens. Tests will simulate the in-situ stress rotation and principal stress axis changes during drawdown, following pre-sheared critical paths identified from preliminary coupled finite element (FE) simulations. The experimental data on anisotropic stiffness and strength will rigorously calibrate a 3D numerical model built using a user-defined material (UMAT) subroutine within a finite element framework (e.g., OpenSees or Abaqus). The UMAT will implement an advanced multi-directional plasticity constitutive model (e.g., a fabric-dependent sand model within the framework of critical state soil mechanics) capable of tracking fabric evolution and stress rotation effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, high-resolution framework linking precise element-scale behavior to field-scale instability predictions for drawdown. It will provide new insights into the controlling mechanisms of fabric-induced anisotropy and stress rotation during failure. The resulting methodology will offer a more robust tool for assessing the stability of slopes under complex, transient hydraulic loading.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, two-way coupling of HCTS tests designed from numerical stress paths with a 3D constitutive model that explicitly accounts for fabric evolution and stress rotation, moving beyond conventional isotropic calibration to capture the true multi-directional nature of drawdown-induced shear.

proposals.title: Element-Level Assessment of Post-Drawdown Slope Stability via Stress History Replication in Cyclic Direct Simple Shear Tests
proposals.motivation: The cyclic stress history imparted by repeated reservoir filling and drawdown is a critical but complex factor in the long-term stability of earth dams and reservoir slopes. Current laboratory practice often simplifies this history, leading to potentially unconservative estimates of post-cyclic shear strength. A more fundamental, element-level methodology that accurately replicates the in-situ stress path is needed to improve safety assessments for aging infrastructure.
proposals.hypothesis: The post-drawdown undrained shear strength of a compacted or natural soil is uniquely governed by its fabric, which is a direct result of its specific multi-stage stress history; replicating this history in the laboratory will yield a more accurate and fundamentally defensible strength parameter for stability analysis than conventional testing approaches.
proposals.method: We will employ a cyclic direct simple shear (DSS) device capable of precise stress path control. For a given soil (e.g., silty sand), we will first consolidate specimens to the estimated in-situ K0 state. A multi-stage loading protocol will then be applied: a static shear stress simulating the slope, followed by a programmed sequence of cyclic shear stress increments and decrements to mimic pore pressure generation and dissipation during successive drawdown and refill events. This 'fatigue' conditioning phase will be designed using coupled finite element analysis (as in Uzuoka's prior work) to translate field-scale loading to the element scale. Finally, a monotonic or cyclic DSS test to failure will be performed on the conditioned specimen to measure the post-drawdown strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, high-fidelity laboratory testing framework for evaluating soil strength after complex stress histories. It will deliver improved input parameters for slope stability models of dams and reservoirs, directly enhancing risk assessment. The methodology also establishes a fundamental link between field-scale loading sequences and element-scale soil response, advancing the science of soil behavior under cyclic loading.
proposals.novelty: The explicit adaptation of material fatigue testing philosophy—pre-conditioning with a simulated service history—to geotechnical element testing is novel. While cyclic tests exist, the systematic, multi-stage replication of a specific field stress path (initial static shear + sequential drawdown cycles) prior to final strength measurement represents a significant advancement over standard cyclic strength or post-cyclic monotonic tests.

proposals.title: Inverse Estimation of Undrained Shear Strength and Permeability of Sloping Saturated Sand Using Seismic and Pore Pressure Response
proposals.motivation: Post-earthquake forensic analysis and performance-based design require accurate in-situ soil strength parameters, which are difficult and costly to obtain directly, especially on slopes. The 2011 Christchurch and 2018 Palu events highlighted the complex interplay of shaking, slope geometry, and pore pressure in triggering failures. A method to back-calculate these parameters from widely recorded seismic and piezometric array data would significantly improve geotechnical assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: The coupled acceleration and excess pore pressure time histories measured at a sloping site during seismic shaking contain sufficient information to inversely estimate key soil parameters, specifically the undrained shear strength and permeability, through a calibrated numerical model.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled finite element model, informed by Uzuoka's prior work, to simulate the dynamics of sloping, saturated sand under seismic loading. The inverse problem will be solved using a Bayesian updating framework (e.g., Markov Chain Monte Carlo) to constrain the posterior distributions of strength and permeability parameters. The likelihood function will be constructed by comparing simulated and measured acceleration and pore pressure responses from centrifuge model tests (providing controlled validation data) and selected field case histories.
proposals.contribution: This work will develop a novel, data-driven framework for non-destructive in-situ parameter estimation, moving beyond traditional back-analysis of final failure geometry. It will provide a quantitative link between dynamic instrumentation data and soil constitutive properties, enhancing the interpretation of seismic monitoring arrays for slope stability assessment.
proposals.novelty: While inverse problems exist in geotechnics, the simultaneous use of both acceleration (a dynamic kinematic field) and pore pressure (a dynamic hydro-mechanical state variable) as coupled constraints for slope-specific strength estimation is a significant advance over methods using only deformation or final failure shape.

proposals.title: Centrifuge Modelling of Seepage-Induced Deformation in Reservoir Embankments on Soft, Deformable Foundations Under Dynamic Shaking
proposals.motivation: The seismic stability of reservoir embankments, such as earth dams and levees, is critically influenced by coupled hydro-mechanical processes involving rainfall/reservoir infiltration, soil liquefaction, and foundation deformation. Pre-2026 practice often simplifies the foundation as rigid or models the reservoir as a static load, neglecting the dynamic pore pressure feedback between a deformable foundation, a realistic reservoir with transient water levels, and the embankment slope during shaking. This gap limits the predictive accuracy for failure mechanisms like settlement, cracking, or slope instability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating a saturated, deformable foundation layer and a reservoir capable of simulating transient seepage and drawdown will significantly alter the dynamic pore pressure response, settlement patterns, and failure mode of a sandy embankment slope during seismic shaking compared to models with a rigid base or static water load.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of centrifuge tests at 50g, constructing a model with a saturated, loose sand embankment on a soft, saturated clay-silt mixture representing a deformable foundation. A realistic reservoir will be modeled using a flexible membrane and a servo-controlled water supply system to simulate rapid drawdown. The model will be instrumented with pore pressure transducers, accelerometers, and displacement markers. Dynamic shaking will be applied using an in-flight actuator. The experimental data will be used to validate a coupled finite element analysis using a u-p-U formulation (considering solid displacement, pore fluid pressure, and fluid displacement), implemented in OpenSees or a similar pre-2026 platform, to capture the relative fluid acceleration and soil-fluid interaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, high-quality experimental dataset on the fully coupled reservoir-embankment-foundation system under dynamic loading. It will deliver a validated numerical modelling framework that practitioners can use to assess seismic performance more reliably. The findings will advance the understanding of how foundation compliance and reservoir dynamics jointly control embankment stability during earthquakes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated physical modelling of a transient reservoir, a deformable embankment, and a soft foundation layer in the centrifuge, a system complexity not addressed in pre-2026 studies of sand reliquefaction or slope infiltration. The coupled numerical analysis explicitly links these experimental advancements to a practical simulation tool.

proposals.title: A Coupled Hydro-Mechanical Framework with Dynamic Permeability and Hysteretic Saturation for Slope Stability Under Seismic and Rainfall Loading
proposals.motivation: The stability of slopes under combined seismic shaking and rainfall is critically governed by the dynamic interaction between soil deformation and pore fluid flow. Pre-2026 models often treat soil permeability and degree of saturation as static or use simplified hysteretic models decoupled from mechanical deformation, limiting predictive accuracy for failure events like flow slides or reliquefaction. A framework that dynamically updates these hydraulic properties based on pressure and deformation is essential for realistic simulation of post-earthquake rainfall infiltration or cyclic loading in unsaturated slopes.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly coupling a hysteretic soil-water retention model with a deformation-dependent permeability function within a dynamic poroelastic finite element analysis will significantly improve the prediction of pore pressure generation, fluid acceleration, and instability in sloping, partially saturated soils under sequential seismic and rainfall loading.
proposals.method: We will extend the researcher's existing coupled finite element code for poroelastic media. The u-p-U formulation will be augmented with (1) a hysteretic SWRC model (e.g., modified van Genuchten) where the scanning paths are updated based on the current suction and deformation-induced void ratio, and (2) a dynamic permeability function where hydraulic conductivity varies with the current degree of saturation and a deformation-dependent intrinsic permeability (e.g., Kozeny-Carman type relation). The framework will be validated against centrifuge test data for sand reliquefaction on slopes and field infiltration data from residual soils.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source numerical tool that captures the synergistic effects of mechanical deformation and hydraulic hysteresis, a key gap in pre-2026 slope stability analysis. It will deliver new insights into the mechanisms of post-seismic rainfall-induced failure and reliquefaction potential in sloping ground, directly informing hazard assessment.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, two-way coupling within a dynamic analysis: deformation alters the SWRC and permeability in real-time, while the evolving hydraulic state feeds back into the effective stress and pore fluid acceleration, moving beyond sequential or one-way coupled approaches common before 2026.

proposals.title: Three-Dimensional u-v-p Finite Element Analysis of Seismic Wave Propagation and Pore Pressure Generation in Sloping, Layered Soil Deposits
proposals.motivation: Current 2D or axisymmetric analyses of soil liquefaction and seismic response, such as those used in centrifuge modeling of pile groups on slopes, often fail to capture complex, three-dimensional wave scattering and pore pressure redistribution. Real-world geotechnical problems, like the interaction of seismic waves with irregular topography, layered residual soils, and embedded structures, are inherently 3D. A robust 3D framework is needed to more accurately assess the risk of slope instability and foundation failure during earthquakes, particularly under varied rainfall and infiltration conditions that create heterogeneous saturation.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the u-v-p (solid displacement, fluid displacement, pore pressure) formulation to full 3D domains will reveal significant directional variations in shear-induced pore pressure generation and wave propagation patterns in sloping, layered ground that are not predictable from 2D simulations, fundamentally altering the assessment of reliquefaction potential.
proposals.method: We will develop a 3D finite element code extending the researcher's prior u-v-p work. The formulation will incorporate the relative fluid acceleration term for dynamic accuracy. The model will simulate layered soil profiles, including unsaturated zones via varying permeability, to study coupled infiltration-seismic loading. Specific algorithms include the generalized-alpha method for time integration and a block-iterative solver for the coupled equations. Validation will use centrifuge test data from prior sand reliquefaction studies, and the model will be applied to a 3D hill slope with subsurface layering.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fully 3D, dynamic u-v-p framework for analyzing seismic soil-fluid-structure interaction in complex topography. It will deliver new insights into the 3D mechanisms of pore pressure redistribution during shaking, leading to improved predictive models for liquefaction-induced slope failures. The resulting open-source code will be a significant tool for advanced geotechnical earthquake engineering research.
proposals.novelty: While 3D FEM exists for solid mechanics, the explicit coupling of full 3D geometry with the advanced u-v-p formulation for dynamic poroelasticity, applied to realistic sloping and layered ground conditions, represents a novel integration beyond the current state-of-the-art 2D or simplified 3D analyses.

proposals.title: Optimal Waveform Design for Seismic Liquefaction Mitigation: A Coupled Poroelasticity and Optimization Framework
proposals.motivation: Earthquake-induced soil liquefaction remains a major geotechnical hazard, causing catastrophic failures of slopes and foundations. Traditional mitigation focuses on ground improvement, which is costly and disruptive. An alternative, underexplored strategy is to modify the dynamic loading itself to minimize pore pressure buildup, offering a potential paradigm shift in seismic risk management for saturated sandy slopes.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given saturated soil profile and slope geometry, there exists an optimal loading frequency and temporal pattern that minimizes excess pore water pressure generation by exploiting destructive interference in the poroelastic wave field and phase relationships between soil skeleton deformation and fluid flow.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled finite element analysis of the dynamics of poroelastic media, incorporating relative fluid acceleration as in Uzuoka's prior work, to model the response of a saturated sand slope. This high-fidelity simulator will be integrated with a numerical optimization loop. Using gradient-based algorithms (e.g., adjoint-state method) or heuristic search (e.g., genetic algorithms), we will iteratively adjust the frequency content and amplitude sequence of the input base motion to minimize a cost function defined by the spatially and temporally integrated excess pore pressure. The centrifuge modelling expertise of the researcher will guide the selection of realistic soil parameters and boundary conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a theoretical and computational framework for 'liquefaction-resistant' ground motion design. It will produce specific guidelines on loading characteristics that suppress pore pressure, contributing to the development of novel seismic isolation or energy dissipation strategies. The findings will advance fundamental understanding of the coupled solid-fluid dynamics under complex loading.
proposals.novelty: While previous research analyzes soil response to given motions, this study inverts the problem: it actively designs the loading as the control variable to achieve a desired geotechnical outcome (pressure minimization). This represents a significant departure from passive analysis towards an active control paradigm in liquefaction engineering.

proposals.title: Seismic Response of Earth Embankments with Realistic, Heterogeneous Initial Moisture Distributions: A Coupled Poroelastic Centrifuge Study
proposals.motivation: Current numerical and physical models for seismic slope stability often assume uniform initial moisture conditions, which is a significant simplification of field reality. Pre-2026 research by Uzuoka and others has established that pore pressure generation and liquefaction are highly sensitive to soil saturation and infiltration patterns. Ignoring natural or construction-induced moisture heterogeneity may lead to non-conservative designs or misinterpretation of failure mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatially heterogeneous initial moisture distributions, driven by rainfall infiltration and slope geometry, will fundamentally alter the dynamic pore pressure response and deformation patterns of sandy embankments during shaking compared to uniform saturation cases.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined centrifuge modelling and advanced numerical simulation approach. First, we will create controlled, repeatable heterogeneous moisture profiles in model sand embankments using pre-shaking infiltration protocols, informed by Uzuoka's prior infiltration work. These models will be subjected to seismic shaking in a geotechnical centrifuge, instrumented with pore pressure transducers and accelerometers. The physical data will validate a coupled finite element analysis using a u-p-U formulation (considering relative fluid acceleration, as in Uzuoka's recent work) to simulate the dynamics of the unsaturated-saturated poroelastic system under seismic loading.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-of-its-kind experimental dataset linking predefined moisture heterogeneity to seismic embankment response. It will yield a validated numerical framework capable of assessing seismic risk for slopes with realistic, non-uniform saturation, directly improving the physical basis for performance-based design in geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: While the influence of uniform saturation degree is known, the intentional creation and systematic study of *spatially heterogeneous* moisture fields as an initial condition for dynamic analysis is novel. It bridges Uzuoka's expertise in infiltration, sloping ground reliquefaction, and advanced poroelastic dynamics in a single, focused experiment.

proposals.title: Coupled Seismic Response of Embankments on Liquefiable Foundations with Flexible Abutments: A Poroelastic Finite Element Investigation
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design of embankments and earth structures often assumes rigid foundation boundaries and non-deformable abutments, which may lead to non-conservative assessments of stability and deformation. In reality, foundations can be compliant and liquefiable, while abutments are flexible, altering the dynamic soil-structure interaction. This gap is critical for infrastructure like highway and railway embankments, where post-earthquake serviceability is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic performance and permanent deformation of an embankment are significantly controlled by the compliance and liquefaction susceptibility of its foundation soil and the flexibility of its lateral boundaries, with rigid boundary assumptions leading to inaccurate predictions of system response.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled finite element analysis using a u-p-U formulation (solid displacement, pore pressure, fluid displacement) to model the dynamics of poroelastic media, incorporating the relative fluid acceleration as in Uzuoka's prior work. The model will simulate a sand embankment on a layered, saturated foundation, with abutments modeled as compliant structural elements. The constitutive model for sand will incorporate a critical state-based formulation capable of simulating cyclic mobility and liquefaction. Centrifuge test data from previous studies on sand reliquefaction will be used for validation of the foundation response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated numerical framework for assessing embankment performance under more realistic boundary conditions. It will quantify the influence of foundation compliance and abutment flexibility on seismic demands, offering improved guidelines for the design and retrofit of embankments in liquefiable environments.
proposals.novelty: While liquefaction and embankment dynamics have been studied separately, the explicit, coupled integration of a compliant, liquefiable foundation with flexible lateral boundary conditions in a dynamic poroelastic analysis represents a significant advancement over conventional rigid-base models.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Seismic Performance Spectrum of Compacted Sand Embankments: A Coupled Centrifuge and Numerical Study on Intermediate Densities and Gradations
proposals.motivation: Current seismic design and analysis for embankments often rely on binary characterizations of soil density (e.g., loose vs. dense), which oversimplifies the continuous spectrum of compaction achievable in the field. This gap is critical because intermediate densities, resulting from variable construction practices or post-construction infiltration, may exhibit non-linear and poorly understood responses to shaking, particularly on slopes. Understanding this continuum is essential for accurate risk assessment of infrastructure like road and rail embankments in seismic zones.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic response (e.g., acceleration amplification, permanent deformation, pore pressure generation) of a sandy embankment is a non-linear, non-monotonic function of its compaction density and gradation, with specific intermediate states potentially exhibiting heightened vulnerability to instability or liquefaction-triggered failure.
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-pronged approach. First, a series of centrifuge model tests will be conducted on instrumented, sloping sand embankments prepared at systematically varied relative densities (e.g., 40%, 55%, 70%, 85%) and gradations (uniform to well-graded), subjected to scaled earthquake motions. Second, the results will be used to calibrate and validate a coupled finite element analysis using a u-p formulation (as in Uzuoka's prior work) that incorporates a critical state-based constitutive model (e.g., PM4Sand). The numerical model will then be used to perform a parametric sweep across the density-gradation continuum, isolating key response metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a foundational dataset and a validated modeling framework that maps seismic performance to a continuous space of compaction states. It will provide engineers with practical guidance on the sensitivity of embankment response to construction quality control and post-construction changes in saturation. The findings will advance beyond the binary loose/dense paradigm in seismic slope stability analysis.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined extremes, this proposal systematically targets the under-explored continuum of intermediate compaction, explicitly linking field-variable density and gradation to coupled dynamic response using physical and numerical modeling validated against each other. It integrates Uzuoka's expertise in centrifuge modeling of slopes, infiltration, and advanced numerical analysis of porous media dynamics.

proposals.title: A Weighted Predictive Model for Seismic Slope Stability: Disentangling the Contributions of Internal Moisture Distribution and External Water Level
proposals.motivation: Current seismic slope stability assessments often treat pore water pressure as a function of a single, static water table, neglecting the complex, transient moisture profiles driven by rainfall infiltration. This simplification is inadequate for slopes in unsaturated residual soils, where internal moisture distribution evolves dynamically, significantly affecting shear strength and liquefaction potential during shaking. A predictive framework that quantifies the relative influence of these two distinct hydrological states is urgently needed for more accurate hazard assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: The seismic stability of a partially saturated slope is governed by a weighted combination of the internal moisture distribution (a transient, unsaturated state variable) and the external water table level (a static, saturated boundary condition), where the weighting shifts based on soil type, infiltration history, and seismic loading characteristics.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled finite element framework, building on Uzuoka's prior work, to simulate the dynamics of unsaturated poroelastic slopes under seismic excitation. A systematic parametric study using centrifuge model data will vary initial moisture profiles (from field infiltration models) and phreatic surface depths. Machine learning (specifically, a Random Forest Regressor or Gradient Boosting model) will be trained on the simulation outputs to identify key features and derive a predictive equation that weights the contributions of the two moisture metrics to a stability index (e.g., factor of safety). The model will be validated against historical case studies of rainfall-triggered slope failures during earthquakes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantifiable metric for assessing how pre-seismic rainfall infiltration modulates seismic risk, moving beyond the binary 'dry' or 'flooded' condition. It will deliver a practical, weighted stability model that can be integrated into regional hazard maps and early warning systems, explicitly accounting for antecedent rainfall. The research will bridge the gap between unsaturated soil mechanics, hydrology, and geotechnical earthquake engineering.
proposals.novelty: While the individual effects of infiltration and liquefaction are well-studied, explicitly decoupling and weighting the concurrent influences of internal moisture distribution and external water level on seismic stability is a novel synthesis. The application of machine learning to derive interpretable weighting functions from high-fidelity coupled simulations represents a significant methodological advance over traditional deterministic or purely empirical approaches.

proposals.title: Inversion of Surface Seismic Response for Subsurface Hydromechanical State: A Coupled Poroelastic Framework for Slope Condition Assessment
proposals.motivation: Pre-failure condition assessment of slopes, especially under rainfall, is critical for landslide hazard mitigation. Current methods often rely on point measurements (e.g., piezometers) which are spatially limited and invasive. A non-invasive technique to map internal moisture and density would provide a transformative, continuous assessment of slope stability, particularly in residual soils and sands prone to liquefaction or failure.
proposals.hypothesis: The dynamic surface response (acceleration and settlement) of a slope to a small seismic event contains sufficient information to inversely characterize the internal, heterogeneous distribution of saturation and soil density through a fully coupled poroelastic formulation.
proposals.method: We will develop a finite element model based on the u-p-U formulation (considering relative fluid acceleration) to simulate the forward dynamics of a sloping, unsaturated poroelastic medium. The inverse problem will be solved using a Bayesian framework, employing Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to estimate posterior distributions of spatially discretized saturation and density parameters. Surface acceleration time histories and permanent settlement from small-scale centrifuge tests (inspired by Uzuoka's prior work) on instrumented slopes with controlled moisture profiles will provide the observational data for inversion and validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational-inverse methodology for non-destructive subsurface characterization. It will advance the field of geotechnical earthquake engineering by explicitly linking small-strain dynamic surface measurements to critical hydromechanical state variables, moving beyond empirical correlations towards physics-based assessment.
proposals.novelty: While forward modeling of coupled dynamics exists, the explicit inversion for moisture/density from surface motion is novel. The integration of a full poroelastic wave propagation model with a Bayesian inverse framework for geotechnical condition assessment represents a significant departure from traditional, simpler back-analysis methods.

proposals.title: Large-Scale Laminar Box Validation of Coupled Seismic Pore Pressure and Slope Response in Unsaturated and Saturated Sands
proposals.motivation: Predicting the seismic performance of slopes and foundations requires understanding the complex interplay between soil skeleton deformation, pore fluid pressure generation, and drainage. While centrifuge tests and advanced numerical models (like coupled finite element analysis considering relative fluid acceleration) have provided critical insights, their validation against large-scale physical models that better capture soil fabric, layer continuity, and boundary effects remains limited. This gap hinders the confident extrapolation of laboratory findings to field-scale geotechnical engineering problems.
proposals.hypothesis: The dynamic response and pore pressure development in sloping sand layers under base shaking, as predicted by advanced centrifuge tests and coupled numerical models, will be validated but will reveal scale-dependent effects related to soil layer thickness and lateral boundaries when tested in a large-scale laminar box apparatus.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of 1-g shaking tests in a large laminar box (e.g., 4m long, 2m high, 1m wide) on instrumented slopes of saturated and partially saturated sand. The test matrix will replicate key conditions from Uzuoka's prior centrifuge work on sand reliquefaction on slopes. Instrumentation will include accelerometers, pore pressure transducers, and displacement sensors. The experimental data will be used to rigorously validate a high-fidelity coupled finite element model, specifically extending the pre-2026 framework that considers the relative acceleration of the pore fluid and solid skeleton, to ensure it captures large-scale boundary and layer interaction effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a crucial benchmark dataset for validating advanced dynamic soil models at a scale closer to field conditions. It will directly quantify the limitations of small-scale centrifuge tests for specific boundary value problems and improve confidence in numerical predictions of seismic slope stability and foundation performance, leading to more reliable design methodologies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, direct comparison of centrifuge-derived reliquefaction behavior and state-of-the-art numerical predictions against large-scale laminar box tests, explicitly targeting the scale effects of soil layer depth and lateral confinement on the coupled hydromechanical response under seismic loading.

proposals.title: Direct Visualization of Pore Fluid Dynamics and Particle Rearrangement in Sloping Sands During Seismic Loading Using 2D Refractive-Index-Matched Micromodels
proposals.motivation: Soil liquefaction during earthquakes remains a critical geotechnical hazard, particularly on slopes where it can trigger devastating lateral spreads. Pre-2026 understanding of the coupled dynamics of pore fluid flow and particle rearrangement, especially under complex conditions like sloping ground and partial drainage, is limited by the inability to directly observe these processes in real soil. This gap hinders the validation of advanced constitutive models for reliquefaction and the assessment of infiltration effects on slope stability post-shaking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the onset of liquefaction and subsequent particle rearrangement in sloping sands is governed by localized, transient pore pressure channels and shear band formation, which can be directly correlated to specific sequences of particle motion and fluid flow patterns observable at the pore scale.
proposals.method: We will fabricate 2D granular micromodels using soft lithography with PDMS, creating a transparent porous network etched with a grain-scale geometry based on a target sand (e.g., Toyoura sand). The pore space and refractive-index-matched fluid (e.g., NaI solution) will enable optical access. The micromodel will be mounted on a tilting stage to simulate slope angles. Seismic loading will be simulated using a programmable, micro-scale shaking table. High-speed particle image velocimetry (PIV) and laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) will be synchronized to track both fluorescent particle trajectories and fluid flow fields simultaneously during dynamic excitation. The experimental data will be used to calibrate and validate a coupled discrete element method (DEM) and lattice Boltzmann method (LBM) simulation of the same granular assembly and flow conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative visualization of the coupled solid-fluid micro-mechanics leading to liquefaction in sloping ground. It will yield a unique dataset linking pore-scale kinematics to macro-scale phenomena like reliquefaction resistance and post-shaking infiltration. The results will serve as a crucial benchmark for validating and improving coupled finite element and poroelasticity codes used for seismic performance assessment.
proposals.novelty: While micromodels have been used for flow studies, their application to dynamically simulate seismic liquefaction under controlled slope conditions with simultaneous, refractive-index-matched measurement of full-field particle and fluid kinematics is novel. This approach directly bridges Uzuoka's expertise in centrifuge reliquefaction tests and coupled finite element analysis with pore-scale experimental validation.

ideas.title: Resonant Pore Pressure Buildup: When Cyclic Loading Makes Seepage More Critical Than Drawdown
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that rapid drawdown is always the most critical condition for slope stability. Drawing an analogy from structural dynamics and fatigue analysis, where resonant cyclic loading accelerates failure, the research investigates if specific seismic shaking patterns can make subsequent steady-state seepage more critical. The analogous solution involves identifying resonant frequencies and modeling cumulative damage. Adapted to geotechnical engineering, the idea is to model whether shaking patterns that match the natural drainage periods of a soil mass can cause a resonant, progressive buildup of pore pressures. This could create a post-earthquake steady-state seepage condition with uniquely high and destabilizing pore pressure distributions, surpassing the criticality of classic rapid drawdown. The investigation would require coupled dynamic-seepage analysis to identify the critical shaking 'patterns' (frequency content, duration, sequencing) that lead to this phenomenon.

ideas.title: Adapting Microfluidic Cell-Scale Assays to Define Soil Constitutive Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from biomedical engineering, where microfluidic platforms are used to apply controlled mechanical and chemical stimuli to individual cells to define their constitutive mechanical properties. The analogous problem in geomechanics is defining the constitutive model for soil under the combined effects of seepage and infiltration, which lead to suction loss. By designing a 'geo-fluidic' element-scale test device, we can precisely control suction, hydraulic gradient, and fluid chemistry independently. This allows for systematic, high-resolution hydromechanical testing to isolate and quantify the soil's constitutive response to each driving factor and their interactions, providing the precise experimental data needed to define and calibrate advanced constitutive models for stability analysis of slopes and embankments.

ideas.title: Calibrating a dynamic infinite slope model with field-scale geophysical monitoring of transient pore pressure fields
ideas.description: This research fuses geophysical field monitoring with mechanistic model refinement to develop and validate a dynamic infinite slope stability framework. Seismic or resistivity monitoring deployed on natural slope analogues provides continuous, spatially distributed data on transient pore pressure fields generated by rainfall infiltration into slopes with pre-existing groundwater. These field-scale data are used to calibrate and test a refined infinite slope model that explicitly incorporates the timing and vertical distribution of pore pressure changes. The outcome is a validated, predictive model that can translate real-time or forecasted rainfall into a dynamic factor of safety, directly linking geophysical signatures to slope stability evolution for improved early warning.

ideas.title: Modeling slope stability using composite material micromechanics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the uniform soil layer assumption in slope modeling by drawing an analogy from materials science, specifically the micromechanics of composite materials. In composites, the macroscopic behavior is derived from the heterogeneous microstructure of fibers and matrix. The analogous solution uses Representative Volume Elements (RVEs) and homogenization theory to upscale these effects. Adapted to geotechnical research, the slope soil is treated as a composite, with heterogeneous inclusions like clay lenses or gravel layers acting as the 'fibers' in a soil 'matrix.' By defining a Soil Representative Volume Element (SRVE) and applying computational homogenization, the research would develop spatially variable effective hydraulic conductivity and shear strength tensors. This provides a physics-based method to model disrupted groundwater flow and infiltration patterns, leading to more accurate predictions of pore pressure development and slope failure in complex, non-uniform soils.

ideas.title: Grain-scale digital twin and reduced-order model for predicting rainfall-induced slope failure timing
ideas.description: This project moves beyond analytical equation refinement by creating a high-resolution digital replica of the centrifuge test slope using a coupled Discrete Element Method (DEM) and pore-scale fluid flow model. This grain-scale model explicitly simulates the dynamic interaction between soil particles, evolving pore structure, and transient groundwater flow, directly capturing the mechanisms that dictate the precise moment of failure initiation observed in experiments. The validated digital twin is then used to generate a comprehensive synthetic dataset, training a physics-informed machine learning model that serves as a practical, reduced-order tool for predicting failure timing from initial soil state and rainfall intensity, bridging the gap between complex micromechanics and field-deployable forecasts.

ideas.title: Paleo-hydrology from Landslide Scars: A Seismic Inversion Approach
ideas.description: This idea applies the inverse problem methodology from seismology to landslide hydrology. In seismology, the source and medium are deduced from surface waves. Here, the landslide scar and its characteristics are the observable outcome. By developing a geomechanical forward model of slope failure driven by pore pressure and rainfall, we can create an inversion system. This system will iteratively adjust the unknown pre-failure groundwater table and rainfall history until the model's simulated failure best matches the real scar's dimensions, morphology, and sedimentological evidence, effectively using the failure to back-calculate the hydrological forcing.

ideas.title: Element-Scale Constitutive Models for Upscaling Failure Acceleration Dynamics
ideas.description: This research fuses high-precision soil element testing with system-scale predictive modeling to create a novel, physics-grounded framework for slope failure. Controlled element-scale tests under combined seepage and infiltration will define a constitutive model for suction loss and fabric evolution. This model is then explicitly upscaled within a computational slope model to predict not just if, but how fast and how extensively a slope will fail due to pre-existing groundwater. The synergy enables the validation of acceleration predictions against the fundamental material response, moving failure forecasting from empirical correlation to a mechanistic, scale-bridging science.

ideas.title: Modeling perched water table dynamics using reservoir cascade and capillary barrier analogs
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the cascading reservoir model from surface hydrology to explicitly model the formation and effect of a perched water table at the soil-bedrock interface on slopes. The analogous solution uses interconnected reservoirs with specific storage and release rules to simulate watershed runoff. Here, the soil profile is conceptualized as a two-reservoir cascade: an upper 'soil moisture reservoir' that fills from antecedent rainfall and a lower 'perched zone reservoir' that activates once the storage at the interface exceeds the capillary barrier's holding capacity. The model will explicitly parameterize the threshold for perched water generation (the barrier's 'spillover' point) and the subsequent downslope flow dynamics within this saturated layer, linking antecedent rainfall inputs directly to changes in pore-water pressure and slope stability.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Classification of Soil-Specific Failure Mechanisms via Distributed Fiber Optic Sensing in Centrifuge Models
ideas.description: This research embeds distributed fiber optic sensing (DFOS) arrays within dynamic centrifuge models of embankments constructed from a strategically selected matrix of soil types, ranging from clean sands to cohesive clays and intermediate silts. The continuous, high-resolution strain profiles captured during failure initiation and propagation will be directly linked to the intrinsic properties and constitutive behaviors of each soil. This approach will reveal how specific soil characteristics control the localization of shear bands, the progression of retrogressive cracking, or the development of liquefaction-induced flows, thereby establishing a mechanistic framework for classifying failure modes. The outcome is a validated, data-rich model that can inform the selection of soil-specific stabilization strategies and improve the accuracy of numerical simulations for slope stability.

ideas.title: Dual problem: design drainage for stabilization
ideas.description: This research expands the original centrifuge testing idea by drawing an analogy from cardiovascular medicine, specifically the design and testing of stents for vascular stabilization. The analogous solution involves using scaled physical models and fluid dynamics analysis to optimize stent geometry for maintaining blood flow and vessel integrity. Adapted to geotechnical engineering, the idea is to use the centrifuge model not only to test drainage effectiveness but to systematically analyze how different drain configurations (treating them as 'soil stents') manage the pore pressure field and seepage forces during simulated failure. This approach aims to move from verifying a given design to actively deriving optimized drainage geometries that ensure long-term slope or foundation stabilization, treating pore water pressure as the analogous fluid to be managed.

ideas.title: Hybrid physical-numerical framework for embankment breach runout prediction from failure initiation to distal deposition
ideas.description: This research creates a robust, validated framework for predicting post-failure debris flow runout by synergistically combining physical and numerical modeling. Centrifuge tests are used to precisely study the stress-controlled breach initiation and the characteristics of the initially mobilized material. These high-quality experimental results then serve as direct input to initialize and calibrate a depth-averaged numerical debris flow model. The calibrated model is subsequently run on high-resolution digital terrain models to simulate the full, long-travel-distance runout and deposition, enabling accurate prediction of inundation zones for risk assessment across realistic, field-scale topography.

ideas.title: Inverse Hydrological Inference from 3D Soil Crack Tomography
ideas.description: This research direction combines high-resolution 3D medical imaging (e.g., X-ray CT) of internal crack networks in frozen landslide debris or laboratory failure samples with inverse modeling techniques. The detailed 3D crack topology, branching, and aperture distribution serve as a physical constraint for hydrological and mechanical models, allowing the backward calculation of the precise groundwater conditions and infiltration history that generated those specific failure patterns. This enables the deduction of not just general pre-failure conditions but the spatial heterogeneity and temporal evolution of soil suction and pore pressure within the failing mass, providing a novel, physically-grounded calibration for landslide hydrology models.

ideas.title: Use fracture mechanics for crack propagation modeling

ideas.title: Material Fatigue Testing via Simulated Service Histories
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodology from metallurgy, where materials are pre-conditioned with simulated service load histories before final failure testing, to the geotechnical problem of assessing soil strength after reservoir drawdown. The analogous solution involves subjecting metal specimens to sequences of cyclic stress that mimic real operational life to study fatigue. Adapted here, soil samples would be pre-sheared in the laboratory under a precise, multi-stage stress path replicating the field history: initial in-situ stresses, followed by sequential cycles of stress change mimicking reservoir filling and drawdown. After this conditioning phase, which imparts the realistic pre-shear and fabric changes, a direct simple shear test is performed to measure the soil's residual or post-cyclic shear strength. This scaled-down, element-level testing protocol provides a more fundamental and accurate prediction of slope or dam stability after repeated drawdown events by replicating the material's stress history at the elemental scale.

ideas.title: Mapping Soil Fabric Evolution to Critical Drawdown Rates via PIV
ideas.description: This research combines a spectrum of controlled drawdown rates with Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) applied to transparent soil or soil-analog models. By tracking the full-field displacement and strain of the soil fabric in real-time during each drawdown test, the study moves beyond simply identifying a failure rate threshold. It directly correlates the onset of failure—such as piping or shear band initiation—with the precise internal kinematic patterns and fabric re-organization that precede it. This creates a mechanistic model for drawdown-induced failure, predicting the critical rate based on the observed evolution of soil microstructure rather than solely on bulk stress or pore pressure changes.

ideas.title: Probabilistic slope stability modeling with geophysically-constrained heterogeneous soil fields
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to model slope stability with heterogeneous soil layers without relying on impossibly detailed direct sampling. It combines electrical resistivity tomography (ERT) and other geophysical methods with a Bayesian data assimilation system to probabilistically characterize subsurface heterogeneity. The approach inverts geophysical data to constrain spatially correlated random fields of hydraulic and mechanical properties, which are then used in coupled hydromechanical slope models. This allows for the quantification of uncertainty in failure predictions and identifies the critical data needed to reduce that uncertainty, making the analysis both more realistic and practically actionable.

ideas.title: High-Resolution Spatiotemporal Mapping of Perched Water Table Dynamics and Slope Deformation via Distributed Fiber Optic Sensing
ideas.description: This research integrates distributed fiber optic sensing (DFOS) directly into physical slope models to capture continuous strain profiles while simultaneously modeling the formation of a perched water table at the soil-bedrock interface. The fiber optic cables, embedded within the soil mass and along the critical interface, provide a high-resolution spatiotemporal map of deformation, allowing researchers to pinpoint exactly where and when strain initiates as the perched water table develops and rises. This setup directly links the hydrological forcing function (antecedent rainfall and perched water pressure) with the resulting geomechanical response, enabling the validation and refinement of hydro-mechanical coupling in slope stability models. The continuous data stream reveals the precise sequence of failure, from initial localized shear to progressive rupture, under the controlled influence of the evolving perched aquifer.

ideas.title: Mechanism-specific forecasting of infrastructure time-to-failure under dynamic groundwater forcing
ideas.description: This research develops mechanism-specific prognostic models that integrate transient, high-resolution groundwater data to forecast the remaining useful life or time-to-failure of geotechnical infrastructure. By focusing on a defined failure mode (e.g., retrogressive landslide, internal erosion), the model explicitly couples the dynamics of the groundwater field—its pressure, flow velocity, and saturation changes—with the mechanical response of the soil or rock mass. The output shifts from generic 'failure acceleration' metrics to probabilistic forecasts of time-to-failure under observed or projected hydrologic conditions, providing actionable lead time for intervention. This approach ensures predictions are physically interpretable and can be validated against instrumented case histories of progressive failure.

ideas.title: Coupled element testing and numerical simulation for drawdown-induced shear
ideas.description: This approach combines targeted advanced laboratory element testing with high-resolution numerical modeling to overcome the limitations of a single test. Laboratory tests on specimens pre-sheared along critical stress paths provide precise data on anisotropic strength and deformation parameters. This data rigorously calibrates a 3D numerical model that simulates the complete, multi-directional stress history and boundary conditions of a representative soil element during drawdown. The coupled system enables the exploration of complex fabric effects and stress rotations that control failure, leading to more robust predictions of field-scale drawdown instability.

ideas.title: High-Resolution PIV-Derived Precursors for Physics-Informed Regional Landslide Early Warning
ideas.description: This research fuses laboratory-scale Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) for soil deformation tracking with regional GIS-based landslide risk modeling. The core innovation is using high-resolution PIV data from controlled tests—which capture pre-failure grain movements and acceleration patterns under varying groundwater conditions—to derive and calibrate new, physically meaningful 'kinematic precursor' parameters for regional models. These calibrated parameters are then integrated into a GIS framework, transforming static susceptibility maps into a dynamic model that can simulate the temporal evolution of slope stability and forecast windows of heightened failure probability at a regional scale, based on the underlying soil mechanics observed at the grain scale.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismological Rupture Metrics for Crack Dynamics in Slopes
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from seismology and earthquake physics to quantify crack initiation and propagation in slopes under rapid drawdown and shaking. The analogous problem—characterizing the initiation and propagation of an earthquake rupture along a fault—is solved using metrics like rupture velocity, stress drop, and radiated energy derived from waveform inversions. The adapted idea is to develop a parallel set of geotechnical crack dynamics metrics (e.g., crack front velocity, crack stress drop, crack energy release rate) by instrumenting physical models or high-fidelity numerical simulations with dense sensor arrays. These sensors would monitor acoustic emissions, strain, and pore pressure, allowing the inversion of data to characterize the full crack evolution process under complex, time-varying loads, providing a more fundamental measure of instability than traditional factor-of-safety approaches.

ideas.title: Characterize Rate-Dependent Crack Kinetics Under Coupled Hydraulic-Seismic Loading
ideas.description: This research systematically tests a continuous spectrum of reservoir drawdown rates, from rapid to quasi-steady, while simultaneously subjecting the system to controlled shaking. For each combined loading scenario, advanced metrics are employed to quantify not just if, but precisely how cracks initiate and propagate—measuring velocity, acceleration, and bifurcation patterns. The synergy creates a novel experimental framework to map failure domains as a function of both drawdown rate and seismic intensity, revealing the specific hydraulic loading path that pushes the system past a stability limit when shaken. This directly informs risk assessment by identifying operational drawdown rates that avoid crossing into a regime of seismic-triggered progressive failure.

ideas.title: Instrumented Element-Scale Testing for 3D Deformation and Suction Field Mapping
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel experimental apparatus that integrates high-resolution internal deformation sensors, such as miniature inclinometer arrays or distributed fiber optic sensing, directly into a modified soil element test chamber (e.g., triaxial or suction-controlled oedometer). The setup subjects a soil specimen to precisely defined coupled hydraulic (seepage and infiltration) and mechanical stress paths. The simultaneous, real-time measurement of internal displacement fields and pore-water pressure/suction at multiple internal points allows for the direct, spatially explicit validation of constitutive model predictions of strain localization and suction loss. This provides unprecedented data to refine and calibrate next-generation hydromechanical models that move beyond homogeneous stress-strain assumptions to capture the intrinsic spatial variability and progressive failure mechanisms within a soil element.

ideas.title: Treating Managed Ecosystems as the Baseline for Stability Analysis
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from structural engineering, where the analytical baseline shifted from unreinforced to code-compliant seismic design. Similarly, in hydrology/geomorphology, we challenge the assumption that a 'rainfall-only' model is the natural baseline. Instead, we establish the 'rainfall with groundwater' case as the new managed baseline, representing a system where natural subsurface storage is actively acknowledged or minimally enhanced. The goal becomes not to model failure from this state, but to explore secondary stabilization methods (e.g., bioengineering, managed aquifer recharge, root reinforcement) required to preserve this baseline's integrity against more extreme climatic forcings. This reframes the research from failure analysis to resilience engineering for a predefined stable state.

ideas.title: Mechanistically-Coupled Predictive Framework for Crack Evolution Under Rapid Drawdown and Seismic Loading
ideas.description: This research develops a unified experimental and modeling framework to predict crack initiation and propagation in earthen structures. It combines high-resolution measurement of crack metrics with simultaneous, co-located sensing of pore pressure, suction, and local strain to establish causative mechanistic links. These integrated datasets are used to calibrate and validate a coupled hydro-mechanical constitutive model capable of simulating crack evolution under complex, time-varying stress paths. The outcome is a predictive tool that moves beyond descriptive metrics to forecast crack development and stability for untested loading sequences and material conditions.

ideas.title: Acoustic Emission Tomography for Spatially Variable Soil Embankments
ideas.description: This research combines acoustic emission (AE) monitoring with the study of embankments constructed with spatially variable or layered soil properties. A dense array of AE sensors is deployed on or within non-homogeneous test embankments to capture micro-cracking events. By analyzing the timing, energy, and location of these acoustic signals, the method creates a 4D (3D space + time) tomographic map of internal damage progression. This allows researchers to directly correlate zones of accelerating micro-fracture activity with known or identified spatial variations in soil strength, stiffness, or layering, fundamentally linking heterogeneity to specific failure pathways.

ideas.title: Apply Stochastic Hydrology Methods from Finance to Model Variable Rainfall-Driven Slope Stability
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the constant rainfall intensity assumption in slope stability analysis. By drawing an analogy from financial engineering, where stochastic models (like geometric Brownian motion) are used to price options under volatile market conditions, we propose a novel adaptation. The analogous solution involves using stochastic differential equations and Monte Carlo simulation to model risk under fluctuating inputs. Adapted to geomorphology, the idea is to treat infiltration as a stochastic process driven by historical or synthetic rainfall time series. This approach would model the transient pore-water pressure response probabilistically, allowing for the computation of time-varying slope failure risk under realistic, variable rainfall patterns, moving beyond deterministic safety factors based on constant worst-case scenarios.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Aftershock Forecasting to Predict Centrifuge Failure Under Hydrological Loading
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from seismology, where machine learning forecasts aftershocks by modeling stress accumulation and transfer in complex earth systems. The analogous problem is solved by training models on sequences of seismic data and physical stress parameters to predict subsequent failure events. Adapted to the research context, we propose training a temporal ML model on centrifuge sensor data, enriched with derived features of accumulated stress and hydrological pore pressure dynamics, to predict the precise timing of mechanical failure under combined hydrological effects, treating the failure as a predictable event in a loaded physical system.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Control-Theory-Driven Landslide Risk Management for Reservoir Operations
ideas.description: This research fuses a GIS-based regional landslide model, incorporating a failure acceleration factor for groundwater conditions, with a precise feedback control system for reservoir drawdown. The synergy creates a closed-loop control framework where the regional susceptibility model provides real-time, spatially distributed risk metrics that act as the primary feedback signal. The controller then dynamically adjusts the reservoir hydrograph in-flight, not just for operational goals, but to actively manage pore-pressure diffusion and slope stability across the vulnerable region. This enables proactive, risk-informed reservoir management that adapts to changing subsurface conditions, moving from static hazard maps to a dynamic risk mitigation system.

ideas.title: Physics-informed machine learning for slope failure prediction using coupled centrifuge-field analogue data
ideas.description: This research fuses controlled centrifuge experiments with field-scale geophysical monitoring to develop a physics-informed machine learning (PIML) framework for slope failure prediction. Machine learning models are first trained on high-resolution centrifuge sensor data to learn the complex, coupled hydrological-mechanical precursors to failure. These models are then adapted and constrained using time-lapse seismic or resistivity data collected from instrumented natural slope analogues, which provide the real-world groundwater-rainfall interactions. The synergy enables the creation of a hybrid digital twin that translates validated centrifuge physics to field conditions, ultimately yielding a predictive tool that can assimilate sparse field monitoring data to forecast failure timing with quantified uncertainty at natural sites.

ideas.title: In-situ 4D X-ray Tomography of Soil Crack Dynamics Under Controlled Environmental Forcing
ideas.description: This approach employs a purpose-built environmental cell compatible with an X-ray micro-CT scanner, allowing for the continuous, non-destructive monitoring of an intact soil specimen. The soil column is subjected to precisely controlled hydraulic or thermal boundary conditions while undergoing sequential tomographic scans. This generates a time-lapse 3D dataset (4D imaging) that captures the complete evolution of the internal crack network, from initiation to stabilization. The method preserves the soil's fabric and stress history, enabling direct correlation between specific environmental drivers and the kinematic development of crack geometry and connectivity.

ideas.title: Apply Geophysical Subsurface Imaging to Track Internal Moisture
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from hydrogeology and environmental geophysics, where time-lapse Electrical Resistivity Tomography (ERT) is routinely used to non-invasively image 3D fluid movement in subsurface aquifers (e.g., tracking contaminant plumes). The analogous solution involves deploying fixed electrode arrays and collecting sequential resistivity datasets to invert for 4D images of saturation and pore fluid changes. Adapted to geotechnical centrifuge testing, a permanent miniature ERT electrode array would be integrated into a centrifuge strongbox. By acquiring rapid ERT scans during spins or at intervals between flights, researchers can generate 4D visualizations of internal moisture movement (both content and salinity) directly correlated with the model's evolving stress state and physical deformation, providing unprecedented insight into internal seepage and failure mechanisms.

ideas.title: Geophysically-constrained 3D modeling of bedrock topography controls on perched aquifer dynamics
ideas.description: This research integrates field geophysical surveys with direct borehole data to create a high-resolution empirical map of the irregular bedrock surface. This mapped topography serves as the dynamic lower boundary condition for a 3D subsurface flow model, moving beyond a static or assumed interface. The system directly tests how specific bedrock features like slopes, depressions, and ridges control the spatial distribution, timing, and persistence of perched water tables, providing a quantifiable, testable link between form and function.

ideas.title: 3D Volumetric Digital Image Correlation with Transparent Soil Surrogates for Internal Granular Deformation
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the opacity of natural soil by employing refractive-index-matched transparent soil surrogates, enabling optical access to the internal grain structure. Sequential 3D image volumes of the deforming specimen are captured using high-resolution X-ray CT or laser-based tomography. A robust 3D Digital Volume Correlation algorithm then analyzes these volumes to track the motion of thousands of individual soil particles or inherent texture patterns, providing a complete 3D displacement and strain field. This allows for the direct, non-invasive measurement of internal soil deformation mechanisms under controlled loading conditions.

ideas.title: Adapting Distributed Acoustic Sensing from Pipeline Monitoring for Slope Stability
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from pipeline integrity monitoring. In the oil & gas industry, Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) uses fiber-optic cables as continuous microphones/seismic sensors to detect, locate, and characterize threats like leaks or ground movement along pipelines over long distances in real-time. The adapted research idea is to install a fiber-optic DAS cable along a natural slope field analogue. This cable would function as a continuous, high-resolution geophysical sensor array, detecting and spatially locating micro-seismic activity and subtle ground strain caused by rainfall-driven pore pressure changes and slope deformation. This provides a much denser spatial and temporal dataset of subsurface hydrological-mechanical interactions than discrete sensors, enabling direct validation of the progressive failure mechanisms and groundwater dynamics observed in centrifuge models.

ideas.title: Applying Geophysical Seismic Tomography to Soil Crack Network Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the geophysical method of seismic tomography to the problem of visualizing internal soil crack networks. In seismology, the propagation of seismic waves is used to image deep Earth structures like faults. The adapted solution involves applying controlled acoustic or ultrasonic pulses to a soil sample or in-situ plot, with an array of receivers capturing the transmitted and reflected signals. By computationally inverting the travel time and amplitude data—much like in geophysical tomography—a 3D image of the crack network's geometry and connectivity can be reconstructed, providing a non-destructive, potentially field-deployable alternative to CT scanning of frozen samples.

ideas.title: Post-Test Rapid-Transfer 3D Imaging Fused with In-Situ Dielectric Sensing for Centrifuge Moisture Tracking
ideas.description: This system overcomes the fatal flaw of instrumenting a centrifuge with bulky medical scanners by implementing a rapid-transfer protocol. Immediately after a centrifuge test at target g-levels, the soil model is automatically and swiftly moved to a waiting, aligned imaging chamber for high-resolution 3D moisture mapping via X-ray CT or MRI, capturing the final moisture distribution. To provide temporal data lost during transfer, an array of miniature, robust dielectric sensors embedded in the sample records local moisture content throughout the centrifuge spin. A data fusion algorithm then combines the continuous sensor timeline with the detailed 3D snapshot to reconstruct the full 4D (3D+time) moisture movement process during the test.

The fiber optics provide the continuous, high-resolution 1D strain profile, while the piezoelectric elements are used to perform frequent, automated active seismic tomography surveys. The concurrent measurement of dynamic strain and evolving 3D seismic velocity structure allows the system to discriminate between different failure modes by correlating localized strain concentrations with specific changes in material stiffness. This integrated approach yields a robust, multi-physics dataset that captures both the location and the mechanistic nature of incipient slope failure.

ideas.title: Define a Sustainable Hybrid Water Budget as the physical baseline for stabilization analysis
ideas.description: This research first establishes a non-arbitrary baseline by defining a Sustainable Hybrid Water Budget (SHWB), calculated as rainfall plus a permissible, renewable fraction of groundwater recharge. All real-world scenarios, including the current 'rainfall with groundwater' case, are then analyzed as deviations from this SHWB baseline. The core investigation focuses on the methods and costs required to stabilize the system back to the SHWB from various over-extraction states, directly linking physical sustainability thresholds to practical stabilization pathways. This approach grounds the analysis in hydrogeological and policy principles, moving beyond a simple inversion of scenarios.

ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics—Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS)—to the problem of measuring internal deformations in geotechnical engineering. In geophysics, DAS uses fiber optic cables as continuous strain sensors to image subsurface faults and seismic activity with high spatial resolution. By embedding similar fiber optic cables within earth structures or soil masses, this adapted research would develop a method for obtaining distributed, high-resolution internal deformation profiles. This approach moves beyond discrete point measurements from instruments like inclinometers, enabling real-time monitoring of shear planes, internal settlement gradients, and localized strain throughout the entire volume of interest.
