ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Interferometry for Distributed Fiber Optic Slope Monitoring
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from seismology. The analogous solution is ambient noise interferometry, where distributed seismic sensors use background vibrations to monitor changes in subsurface properties. We adapt this by embedding distributed fiber optic cables (DFOS) in a slope model to obtain continuous, high-resolution dynamic strain profiles from ambient vibrations. By applying interferometric processing to these DFOS data streams, we can continuously compute and monitor baseline correlation functions. Temporal changes in these functions will reveal evolving internal stiffness and stress redistribution within the slope, offering a novel, highly sensitive method for detecting the precursors to slope failure long before major strain localization occurs.

ideas.title: Robust Adaptive Control for Reservoir Drawdown Under Hydrological Uncertainty
ideas.description: This research develops a control system for programming reservoir hydrographs that is explicitly designed to handle the inherent uncertainties of subsurface hydrology. It combines robust control techniques, which guarantee performance within known error bounds, with adaptive algorithms that continuously update the system's model based on real-time drawdown observations. This hybrid approach ensures stable and accurate tracking of complex target hydrographs even with imperfect knowledge of storage characteristics, recharge, and boundary flows. The result is a practical and resilient methodology for managed aquifer recharge and reservoir management that functions reliably in real-world conditions.

ideas.title: Model Predictive Control for Adaptive Reservoir Drawdown
ideas.description: This research adapts Model Predictive Control (MPC), a advanced feedback method from chemical process engineering, to the problem of programming complex reservoir drawdown hydrographs. In chemical plants, MPC uses a dynamic model to predict process behavior and solves a rolling-horizon optimization to determine control actions that precisely track a desired output trajectory while adhering to operational constraints. Adapted to reservoir management, a hydrological model (e.g., coupling reservoir storage dynamics with downstream routing) would be used within an MPC framework. At each decision step (e.g., hourly), the controller would optimize planned gate operations over a look-ahead period to minimize deviation from a target downstream hydrograph, subject to constraints on gate actuation speeds, minimum environmental flows, and reservoir safety levels. Only the immediate gate adjustments are implemented before the cycle repeats with updated measurements, enabling 'in-flight' correction of the drawdown to achieve realistic, pre-programmed discharge patterns despite uncertainties in inflows or system parameters.

ideas.title: Adapting Material Fatigue Testing to Determine Portfolio Drawdown Failure Thresholds
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from materials fatigue testing, where an S-N curve is used to find a material's endurance limit, to the problem of determining portfolio drawdown sufficiency. In the analogous field, materials are subjected to repeated stress cycles at controlled amplitudes to find the stress threshold for failure. The adapted research idea applies this by treating a retirement portfolio as the 'material,' withdrawal rates as the 'stress amplitude,' and the sequence of returns (or time horizon) as the 'cycles.' The study would test a spectrum of drawdown rates and sequence patterns to generate a financial 'S-N curve,' aiming to empirically identify a probabilistic failure threshold or 'endurance limit.' This directly challenges the assumption that a single static drawdown rate (like the 4% rule) is sufficient by demonstrating how the 'failure point' varies with the rate's magnitude and the sequence of its application.

ideas.title: Adaptive Acoustic Emission Monitoring with Pattern Recognition for Early Failure Prediction
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from seismology, where networks of sensors detect and analyze foreshock patterns to forecast major earthquakes, to the problem of structural failure prediction. The analogous solution involves using dense sensor arrays and pattern recognition algorithms to interpret precursor event sequences for probabilistic forecasting. The adapted research idea applies this framework to acoustic emission (AE) monitoring by deploying a dense AE sensor network on critical infrastructure or materials under test. Machine learning algorithms will be developed to analyze the spatial clustering, temporal evolution, and energy characteristics of micro-cracking AE events. This transforms AE monitoring from a passive detection tool into an active prognostic system capable of providing a quantitative, time-sensitive probability of macroscopic failure, enabling predictive maintenance and safety interventions.
